As long as the inference is not complete for him, he is not certain of anything nor settled upon anything; rather, he is an assumer and estimator of what he has assumed and estimated.

Accordingly, Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) saying regarding the star, "This is my lord," and likewise his upcoming saying regarding the moon and the sun, is not of the certainty and settlement that are counted as polytheism (الشرك). Rather, it is the assumption of a matter to look at the signs that affirm and support it. Evidence for this is the clarity in the verses that he (peace be upon him) was in a state of anticipation and waiting. This is one perspective.

However, what is supported by what Allah narrated from him in Surah Maryam in his argument with his father: **يَا أَبَتِ إِنِّي قَدْ جَاءَنِي مِنَ الْعِلْمِ مَا لَمْ يَأْتِكَ فَاتَّبِعْنِي أَهْدِكَ صِرَاطًا سَوِيًّا * يَا أَبَتِ لَا تَعْبُدِ الشَّيْطَانَ إِنَّ الشَّيْطَانَ كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَنِ عَصِيًّا * يَا أَبَتِ إِنِّي أَخَافُ أَنْ يَمَسَّكَ عَذَابٌ مِنَ الرَّحْمَنِ فَتَكُونَ لِلشَّيْطَانِ وَلِيًّا * قَالَ أَرَاغِبٌ أَنْتَ عَنْ آلِهَتِي يَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهِ لَأَرْجُمَنَّكَ وَاهْجُرْنِي مَلِيًّا * قَالَ سَلَامٌ عَلَيْكَ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي إِنَّهُ كَانَ بِي حَفِيًّا** — "O my father! Indeed there has come to me a knowledge that has not come to you. So follow me, I will guide you to a right path. O my father! Do not worship Satan. Indeed Satan is disobedient to the All-beneficent. O my father! I fear lest a punishment from the All-beneficent should strike you, and you should become a confidant of Satan. He said, ‘Are you turning away from my gods, O Ibrahim? If you do not desist, I will surely stone you! Now leave me for a long time.’ He said, ‘Peace be to you! I will plead with my Lord to forgive you. Indeed He is gracious to me’" (19:43-47)—is that he (peace be upon him) was already knowledgeable of the reality of the matter, and that the One who takes charge of managing his affairs, who is gracious to him and exceeds in honoring him, is Allah, the Glorified, and none other.

Based on this, his saying "This is my lord" follows the course of concession (التسليم) and going along (المجاراة), placing himself as one of them, going along with them and conceding what they conceded, then clarifying what makes the corruption of their view and the falsehood of their claim apparent. This method of argumentation is more conducive to the opponent's fairness, more preventive of the eruption of his partisanship and zeal, and more suitable for making the proof heard.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَمَّا أَفَلَ قَالَ لَا أُحِبُّ الْآفِلِينَ** — "Then when it set, he said, ‘I do not like those who set.’" Setting (الأفول) is vanishing. In it is the invalidation of the lordship of the star by the occurrence of the attribute of setting to it. For the setting star is cut off by its setting from those upon whom it rose, and no cosmic management (تدبير كوني) can be consistent with such disconnection.

Furthermore, Lordship (الربوبية) and being nurtured (المربوبية) exist through a real connection between the Lord and the nurtured, which leads to the nurtured's love for its Lord due to its ontological attraction (انجذابه التكويني) toward Him and its dependency upon Him. There is no meaning to the love of that which perishes and changes from its beauty for which the love existed. As for what is observed of man loving much of the fleeting beauty and the vanishing adornment, it is only due to his immersion in it without paying attention to its perishing and cessation. It is necessary that the Lord be of stable existence, not changing in states like these decorated ornaments that live and die, stay and vanish, rise and set, appear and hide, grow young and grow old, flourish and wither. This is a demonstrative (برهاني) perspective, even if it might be imagined to be a rhetorical or poetic explanation; so understand that.

In any case, he (peace be upon him) invalidated the lordship of the star by the occurrence of setting to it, either by using it as a metonymy for falsehood—that he does not love it because of its setting, since being nurtured and servitude are constituted by love, so it is not possible for one who does not love a thing to worship it. It has been narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Is religion anything but love?" and we have explained this previously.

Or, it is because the argument is constituted by the absence of love, and the setting was mentioned only to justify his lack of love for it, which is incompatible with Lordship. This is because Lordship and Divinity necessitate being beloved; thus, that to which the instinctive, primordial love (الحب الغريزي الفطري) does not attach due to its lack of lasting, stable beauty does not deserve Lordship. This perspective is the apparent one upon which the context of the argumentation in the verse relies.

In the discourse, there is first an indication of the correlation between love and servitude or being worshipped.

Secondly, he took a common attribute between the star, the moon, and the sun—setting—to invalidate lordship, then he carried out the argumentation and repeated what he argued regarding the star for the moon and the sun as well. This was either because he (peace be upon him) had no prior knowledge of the moon and the sun and that they rise and set like the star, as previously indicated, or because the people addressed in each of the three stages were different from the others.

Thirdly, he chose for the negation the description of rational beings (أولي العقل) when he said: **لَا أُحِبُّ الْآفِلِينَ** — "I do not like those [rational beings] who set." It is as if to indicate that those devoid of consciousness and intellect do not deserve Lordship from the outset, as alluded to in his narrated saying: **يَا أَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ مَا لَا يَسْمَعُ وَلَا يُبْصِرُ وَلَا يُغْنِي عَنْكَ شَيْئًا** — "O my father! Why do you worship what neither hears nor sees, and is of no avail to you in any way?" (19:42). And his other saying: **إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ * قَالُوا نَعْبُدُ أَصْنَامًا فَنَظَلُّ لَهَا عَاكِفِينَ * قَالَ هَلْ يَسْمَعُونَكُمْ إِذْ تَدْعُونَ * أَوْ يَنْفَعُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَضُرُّونَ * قَالُوا بَلْ وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا كَذَلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ** — "When he said to his father and his people, ‘What do you worship?’ They said, ‘We worship idols, and are constant in our devotion to them.’ He said, ‘Do they hear you when you call them? Or do they benefit you, or harm you?’ They said, ‘No, but we found our fathers acting likewise’" (26:70-74). He first asked them about their object of worship as if he knew nothing of them, so they answered with what suggested they are bodies and structures without intellect or consciousness. Then he asked them about their knowledge and power, using the plural form for rational beings to indicate that the worshipped must possess this attribute—the attribute of intellect.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَمَّا رَأَى الْقَمَرَ بَازِغًا قَالَ هَذَا رَبِّي** — "Then when he saw the moon rising, he said, ‘This is my lord.’" Rising (البزوغ) is appearing. The discussion regarding the indication of His saying "Then when he saw..." etc., on the connection of the incident to what preceded has already passed. And His saying "This is my lord" is by way of assumption or going along and conceding, similar to what preceded in the previous verse.

As for his saying after the setting of the moon: **لَئِنْ لَمْ يَهْدِنِي رَبِّي لَأَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الضَّالِّينَ** — "If my Lord does not guide me, I will surely be among the people who are astray," it is placed as a metonymy. He (peace be upon him) invalidated the lordship of the star with that which encompasses every vanishing thing. When the moon set, his view regarding its lordship then appeared as what he had said before regarding the star: "I do not like those who set." Thus, his saying "If my Lord does not guide me..." etc., is an indication that the position he mentioned regarding the moon by saying "This is my lord" would be misguidance (ضلال) if he were to persist and insist upon it, and he would be one of those astray who claim its lordship. The reason for it being misguidance is what he said regarding the star, where he expressed it with a description that is not exclusive to it but holds true in its case and every case similar to it.

In the discourse, there is first an indication that there was a people there who claimed the lordship of the moon just like the star, just as his saying in the following verse after mentioning the sun: **يَا قَوْمِ إِنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ** — "O my people! I am indeed quit of what you take as [His] partners" is not devoid of indicating the same.

Secondly: He (peace be upon him) was at that time on the path of seeking, hoping for divine guidance, anticipating what his Lord would bestow upon him of correct insight and certain view—whether that was in reality, as if we take the discourse as an assumption to attain belief, or in appearance, as if we take it as a concession to clarify corruption. Both perspectives have been mentioned.

Thirdly: He (peace be upon him) was certain that he had a Lord to whom the management of his guidance and all his affairs belonged. He was merely searching—really or apparently—to know Him: is He the very One who originated the heavens and the earth, or one of His creatures? When it became clear to him that the star and the moon were not fit for Lordship due to their setting, he expected his Lord to guide him to Himself and save him from the misguidance of those who are astray.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَمَّا رَأَى الشَّمْسَ بَازِغَةً قَالَ هَذَا رَبِّي هَذَا أَكْبَرُ** — "Then when he saw the sun rising, he said, ‘This is my lord! This is bigger!’" The discussion regarding the indication of the wording on the connection to what preceded—due to the word "Then when" (فَلَمَّا)—and the fact that his saying "This is my lord" was intended for assumption or concession, is as preceded in the previous verse.

His saying "This is my lord" was repeated regarding the moon when he saw it rising after seeing the star. Therefore, he added his saying "This is bigger" to his saying "This is my lord" regarding the sun the third time, to serve as an excuse for returning to the assumption of lordship for it despite the error of his assumption becoming clear time after time.

It was previously indicated that his pointing to the sun with the masculine demonstrative "this" (هَذَا) suggests that he (peace be upon him) did not know of the sun what one of us knows—that it is a celestial body that rises and sets according to the apparent sense of the senses every day and night, and to which the day, the night, and the four annual seasons are attributed, among other of its attributes.

For using the masculine demonstrative is what one who does not distinguish the type of the thing pointed to resorts to, just as you say of someone whose silhouette appears to you while you do not know if it is a man or a woman: "Who is this (هذا)?" Similarly, it is said of a silhouette when it is not known if it is of the rational beings or not: "What is this (هذا)?" Perhaps this was from Ibrahim (peace be upon him) when he first emerged from the hiding place in which he was concealed to his father and his people, and he had no experience of witnessing the external world and human society. So he saw a body that was a star, a body that was the moon, and a body that was the sun. Every time he witnessed one of them—and he only witnessed a luminous, shining body—he said: "This is my lord," by way of not knowing its state with complete knowledge, as you have heard.

This is somewhat supported by his saying: **فَلَمَّا أَفَلَ قَالَ لَا أُحِبُّ الْآفِلِينَ** — "Then when it set, he said, ‘I do not like those who set.’" For in it is an indication that he (peace be upon him) remained settled upon the star being a lord until he witnessed its setting, then he judged that the assumption was false and that it was not a lord. Had he known that it would set, he would have invalidated its lordship concurrently with his assumption of its lordship, as he did in the matter of the idols, as indicated by his saying to his father: **أَتَتَّخِذُ أَصْنَامًا آلِهَةً إِنِّي أَرَاكَ وَقَوْمَكَ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُبِينٍ** — "Do you take idols for gods? Indeed I see you and your people in manifest error" and also his saying: **يَا أَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ مَا لَا يَسْمَعُ وَلَا يُبْصِرُ وَلَا يُغْنِي عَنْكَ شَيْئًا** — "O my father! Why do you worship what neither hears nor sees, and is of no avail to you in any way?"

Though it is possible to say: he intended by delaying his saying "I do not like those who set" until it set to argue with them through what the senses perceived, just as he intended by what he did with the idols—when he made them into fragments except for the biggest of them—to show them the helplessness of the idols and their being dead bodies that cannot repel harm or evil from themselves.

The commentators have various ways of justifying the use of the masculine demonstrative in His saying: **هَذَا رَبِّي هَذَا أَكْبَرُ**:

Some said: The masculine demonstrative is by way of interpreting the thing pointed to as "the celestial luminous body" (الجرم النير السماوي), meaning: "this thing pointed to" or "this luminous body is my lord and it is bigger."

The objection to this is that while there is no doubt about the correctness of usage with this interpretation, the issue lies in the subtle point (نكتة) that justifies this interpretation. This is not permissible without a justifying point. If it were permissible in the language without relying on a point, it would be permissible to masculinize every feminine noun—whether by rule or by usage—in referring back with a pronoun or demonstrative by interpreting it as "the person" or the like, and in that is the definitive abrogation of the language.

Others said: It is of the type of the subject (المبتدأ) following the predicate (الخبر) in its masculinity. Since "Lord" (رب) and "bigger" (أكبر) are masculine, the demonstrative followed the masculine predicate, just as the opposite occurred in His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قَالُوا** — "Then their only excuse will be to say..." (6:23), where the masculine followed the feminine.

The objection to this is that they used to see among the deities females just as they posited males, and they called the female among the deities a goddess (إلهة), a lady (ربة), a daughter of God, and a wife of the Lord. Thus, it would have been necessary to refer to the sun as a lady (ربة) due to its femininity, and to say: "This (هذه) is my lady (ربتي) or my goddess (آلهتي)." So the discussion regarding the masculinity of the predicate in His saying "This is my lord" is like the discussion regarding the masculinity of the subject; there is no meaning then to the talk of following (الإتباع).

Likewise, His saying **هَذَا أَكْبَرُ** — "This is bigger." The predicate here is of the elative forms (صيغ التفضيل), and the rule for the elative form when it occurs as a predicate is that it comes as *af'al* (أفعل) and remains the same for masculine and feminine. It is said: "Zayd is better (أفضل) than 'Amr" and "Layla is more beautiful (أجمل) than Salma." That which is of this nature, we do not concede that it is of the masculine forms in which following (الإتباع) occurs.

Others said: The masculinity of the demonstrative is only to glorify the sun, as he attributed lordship to it, protecting the deity from the blemish of femininity.

The objection to this is: they used to count femininity among the deficiencies from which the deity must be sanctified, yet the people of Babylon themselves had female deities, such as the goddess "Ninu," the goddess of the succeeding mothers; the goddess "Nin-Karasha," the daughter of the god "Anu"; the goddess "Malkat," the wife of the god "Shamash"; the goddess "Zarpanit," the goddess of nursing; and the goddess "Anunnaki." A group of the Arab polytheists used to worship the angels and count them as the daughters of Allah. They narrated in the interpretation of His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلَّا إِنَاثًا** — "They invoke none but females besides Him" (4:117) that they used to call their deities females, and they would say: "The female of the children of so-and-so," meaning the idol they worshipped.

Others said: Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) people used to count the sun as male, and they posited for it a wife they called "Anunit." Thus, the discourse preserved the apparent sense of their belief.

The objection to this is: their belief in the sun being male does not justify changing the femininity of its word to masculinity. Furthermore, his saying (peace be upon him) to the king: **فَأْتِ بِهَا مِنَ الْمَغْرِبِ** — "then bring it from the west" (2:258), referring to the sun, contradicts that.

Others said: Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was speaking in the Syriac language, which was the language of his people, and in it, no distinction is made in pronouns and demonstratives between masculine and feminine; rather, all are in the masculine form. The Noble Quran, in narrating his saying, preserved the masculinity he used.

The objection to this is: the denial of the permissibility of that. For it is a matter pertaining to the rules of words which differ with the difference of languages. Rather, that is only permissible in what pertains to the meaning which does not affect the linguistic specificity. Moreover, He, the Exalted, narrated from Ibrahim (peace be upon him) many arguments and abundant supplications in the Quran, and in them are many instances where femininity was considered. Why then was this instance specifically singled out for the cancellation of the aspect of femininity? Even his saying in what he argued with the king of Babylon: **إِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ رَبِّيَ الَّذِي يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ قَالَ أَنَا أُحْيِي وَأُمِيتُ قَالَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْتِي بِالشَّمْسِ مِنَ الْمَشْرِقِ فَأْتِ بِهَا مِنَ الْمَغْرِبِ فَبُهِتَ الَّذِي كَفَرَ** — "When Ibrahim said, ‘My Lord is He who gives life and makes to die,’ he said, ‘I [too] give life and make to die.’ Ibrahim said, ‘Indeed Allah brings the sun from the east; so you bring it from the west.’ Thereat the faithless one was confounded" (2:258), includes the mention of the sun and the femininity of the pronoun returning to it.

Among the strange things brought against this perspective is what some commentators mentioned and insisted upon: that Ibrahim (peace be upon him), as well as Isma'il and Hajar, used to speak the ancient Arabic language, and that it was the language of his people. He said, in summary: It has been established among scholars of ancient archaeology that the Arabs of the Peninsula colonized the lands of the Chaldeans and Egypt since the dawn of history, and their language prevailed in both. Some stated that King Hammurabi, who was a contemporary of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), was an Arab. This Hammurabi was the king of righteousness and peace, described in the Old Testament as the priest of God Most High; it is mentioned therein that he blessed Ibrahim, and that Ibrahim gave him a tenth of everything. He said: It is well known in the books of Hadith and Arab history that Ibrahim settled his son Isma'il (peace be upon him) with his mother Hajar the Egyptian in the valley where Mecca was built afterward, and that Allah subjected for them a group from Jurhum who lived with them there. Ibrahim (peace be upon him) used to visit them, and he and his son Isma'il built the Sacred House of Allah and spread the religion of Islam in the Arab lands. In the Hadith: Ibrahim (peace be upon him) came to Mecca to visit his son—who had gone out to hunt—so he spoke to his wife, who was from Jurhum, but he did not approve of her state. Then he came to her after a period to visit him but did not find him, so he spoke to his other wife—who invited him to dismount and washed his head—so he approved of her state and prayed for her. All of this indicates that he used to speak Arabic. This is the summary of what he mentioned.

The objection to this is: the proximity of the Arabs of the Peninsula to Egypt and Chaldea and their mixing with them, or their colonization and dominance over them, does not necessitate the change of their languages to Arabic. The language of Egypt was Coptic, and the language of the Chaldeans and Assyrians was Syriac. Yes, that might have necessitated the entry of names and words from the language of some into the language of others, as is found in the Noble Quran such as *al-qistas* and *al-istabraq* and others, which are loanwords.

As for what he mentioned regarding Hammurabi and his contemporaneity with Ibrahim (peace be upon him), it does not match what is correct of his history. This is supported by the artifacts uncovered from the ruins of Babylon and the excavated steles on which his code was written—which he established and implemented in his kingdom, and it is the oldest codified law in the world according to what has reached us. Some have mentioned that the days of his reign were between 1728 BCE and 1686 BCE, while others mentioned that he ruled Babylon in the years 2287–2232 BCE. Ibrahim (peace be upon him) lived around 2000 BCE. This Hammurabi was a pagan; he sought help—in what was found of his words engraved at the end of his code engraved on the stele after him—from the deities for the permanence of his code, for people to act upon it, and for the destruction of whoever intended to abrogate it or oppose it. As for what he mentioned of the report of his settling his son and the mother of his child in Tihama, the building of the Sacred House of Allah, the spreading of the religion of Allah, and his communication with the Arabs, none of that indicates that he (peace be upon him) was a speaker of the Arabic language, and that is apparent.

Let us return to where we were and say: His saying: **فَلَمَّا رَأَى الشَّمْسَ بَازِغَةً** etc.—as you have heard—indicates the connection of what follows "Then when" (فَلَمَّا) with what preceded it, which is His saying: **فَلَمَّا أَفَلَ قَالَ لَئِنْ لَمْ يَهْدِنِي رَبِّي لَأَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الضَّالِّينَ**. It thus indicates that the moon had already set when he (peace be upon him) saw the sun rising. This only occurs in autumn or winter in the northern latitudes where the lands of the Chaldeans were, when the nights are long, especially if the moon is in one of the southern zodiac signs like Sagittarius or Capricorn. At that time, the celestial position allows the moon to set in the latter half of the lunar month before sunrise. It was previously mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَمَّا جَنَّ عَلَيْهِ اللَّيْلُ رَأَى كَوْكَبًا قَالَ هَذَا رَبِّي فَلَمَّا أَفَلَ قَالَ لَا أُحِبُّ الْآفِلِينَ * فَلَمَّا رَأَى الْقَمَرَ بَازِغًا قَالَ هَذَا رَبِّي** that the apparent sense of the discourse, supported by rational consideration, indicates that the night was one of the nights of the latter half of the lunar month, and the star was Venus. He witnessed it first in the west in its descent, then witnessed its setting and the rising of the moon from the direction of the east.

It is thus derived from the verses that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) argued with his people regarding the idols on the day he argued with them, and he remained occupied with them that day until the night darkened over him. When the night darkened over him, he saw Venus and a people worshipping it, so he went along with them regarding its lordship and began waiting for what state would befall it until it set after a few hours. Then he argued with them through it and disavowed its lordship. Then he saw the moon rising, and there was a people worshipping it, so he went along with them regarding its lordship by saying: "This is my lord," and began monitoring what would happen to it until it set. The night was one of the long nights in the second half of the lunar month, and perhaps the moon was traveling in a short arc of the southern orbital arcs. When it set, he disavowed its lordship and began seeking guidance from his Lord and seeking refuge in Him from misguidance until the sun rose. He saw it rising and bigger relative to what preceded it of the star and the moon. He returned likewise to going along with them regarding its lordship, despite what had appeared to him of the falsehood of the lordship of the star and the moon—which are like it in being luminous celestial bodies—but he took its being bigger than them as an excuse to justify what he assumed or conceded of its lordship, saying: "This is my lord! This is bigger!" He began waiting for the future of the matter until it set, then he disavowed its lordship and the polytheism of his people, saying: "O my people! I am indeed quit of what you take as [His] partners." Then he affirmed Lordship for Allah, the Glorified, just as he used to affirm Divinity in the sense of bringing the heavens and the earth into existence and originating them for Him, the Exalted, saying: **إِنِّي وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِيَ** — which is servitude (العبودية) in the face of Lordship — **لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ حَنِيفًا** — not deviating from the very center to the right or left — **وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — by associating anything of His creation and His originated beings with Him, the Exalted, in worship and submission.

It was previously mentioned that His saying, the Exalted, within the verses and surrounded by them: **وَكَذَلِكَ نُرِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ** indicates that he (peace be upon him) only took the argument he presented to his father and his people from what he witnessed of the dominion (ملكوت) of the heavens and the earth. Allah, the Glorified, poured the certainty (اليقين) which He mentioned as the goal for showing the dominion upon his heart through this witnessing and vision.

This is the clearest witness that the argument he (peace be upon him) mentioned was a demonstrative argument (حجة برهانية) that nursed from the breast of certainty. In that, he brought his saying: **لَا أُحِبُّ الْآفِلِينَ**, and the formulation of its proof has been previously indicated.

From all that has preceded, it has become clear:

**First:** That his saying (peace be upon him): **لَا أُحِبُّ الْآفِلِينَ** is a demonstrative, certain argument (حجة برهانية يقينية) in which the discourse was built upon his lack of love for those who set, and the incompatibility of setting with Lordship. It appears from the words of some that they take it as a commoner's argument (حجة عامية), not a demonstrative one, as they say: "The correct view is that the discourse was a subtle exposure, not a clear discursive proof, in which he exposed the ignorance of his people in worshipping the stars—that they worship what hides from them and knows nothing of their worship. This is the reason for making setting incompatible with Lordship rather than rising and appearing; rather, he built the claim of Lordship upon the latter, for among the attributes of the Lord is to be manifest, even if His manifestation is not like the manifestation of others among His creatures." This is what they said.

They missed, firstly: that the placement of His saying: **وَكَذَلِكَ نُرِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ** between the verses containing his arguments is the greatest evidence that his arguments were taken from his witnessed dominion-realities (مشهوداته الملكوتية) which are the criterion for his certainty in Allah and His signs. How can it be imagined, along with that, that it is a commoner's argument and not a demonstrative one?

They missed, secondly: that the argument was built upon love and its absence, not upon setting alone. Additionally, building upon setting also does not remove it from being demonstrative. He (peace be upon him) only mentioned the reason for his disavowal of their lordship: that he found them setting and vanishing, and he does not love those who set, so he does not worship them. It is well known that a human's worship of his Lord is only because He is a Lord—that is, because He manages the human's affairs, bestowing upon him life, sustenance, health, fertility, security, power, and knowledge, among other things he needs for his survival. Thus, he is dependent in existence upon his Lord from every aspect. It is among the primordial instincts of man to love what makes him happy among the things he needs, and to love the one who makes him happy through that; no doubter doubts this at all. Servitude to the Lord only exists because man loves Him for bringing benefits to him, or for repelling harms from him, or for both together.

It is also among the primordial instincts of man that his soul does not attach to that which has no permanence, unless greed or lust or the like turns his gaze toward the side of pleasure and distracts him from reflecting and contemplating the side of its perishing and cessation. The Noble Quran has used this method frequently in censuring the world and deterring people from excessive attachment to its adornment and immersion in its desires, such as His saying: **إِنَّمَا مَثَلُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا كَمَاءٍ أَنْزَلْنَاهُ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهِ نَبَاتُ الْأَرْضِ مِمَّا يَأْكُلُ النَّاسُ وَالْأَنْعَامُ حَتَّى إِذَا أَخَذَتِ الْأَرْضُ زُخْرُفَهَا وَازَّيَّنَتْ وَظَنَّ أَهْلُهَا أَنَّهُمْ قَادِرُونَ عَلَيْهَا أَتَاهَا أَمْرُنَا لَيْلًا أَوْ نَهَارًا فَجَعَلْنَاهَا حَصِيدًا كَأَنْ لَمْ تَغْنَ بِالْأَمْسِ** — "The parable of the life of this world is that of water which We send down from the sky: it mingles with the earth’s vegetation from which humans and cattle eat. When the earth puts on its luster and is adorned, and its people think they have power over it, Our edict comes to it by night or day, and We turn it into a mown field, as if it had not flourished the day before" (10:24). And His saying: **مَا عِنْدَكُمْ يَنْفَدُ وَمَا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ بَاقٍ** — "What is with you will be exhausted [but] what is with Allah is lasting" (16:96). And His saying: **وَمَا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَى** — "and what is with Allah is better and more lasting" (42:36).

Thus, he (peace be upon him) conveys by his saying "I do not like those who set" that that which is of such a nature that man loses it and it vanishes from him, and neither remains nor stays for him, does not deserve for man to love it and for his soul to attach to it. The Lord whom man worships must be loved by him; therefore, He must not be of such a nature that He sets away from him and is lost. Thus, these setting bodies do not deserve the name of Lordship. This—as you see—is an argument known to both the common folk and the elite.

Thirdly, they confused rising (البزوغ) with manifestation (الظهور), judging that it is not incompatible with Lordship, but rather the claim is built upon it—that among the attributes of the Lord is to be manifest, until the end of what they said. For what was mentioned in the verse—and the argument was not built upon it—is rising (البزوغ), which is appearing and manifestation after hiddenness, which is incompatible with Lordship. So the question remains: why was the discourse built upon setting rather than rising?

**Second:** That taking setting in the argument rather than rising is only because rising does not necessitate the absence of love upon which the argument was built, unlike setting. By this, what is in the saying of *al-Kashshaf* regarding the reason for the turn (العدول) becomes clear. He said: "If you say: Why did he argue against them with setting rather than rising, when both are a transition from one state to another? I say: Arguing with setting is more manifest because it is a transition accompanied by hiddenness and veiling." End quote. For the argumentation, as you have known, is only by the absence of the attachment of love, not by setting itself, such that the turn from rising to it would be justified by what he mentioned.

**Third:** That the argumentation was only intended to negate the lordship of the three bodies in the sense of their management of the terrestrial world or the human world, not Lordship in the sense of the station to which both origination and management ultimately return. For the pagans and the worshippers of the stars do not deny that their deities are not lords in this sense, and that He is Allah, the One, who has no partner.

From here, what is in the saying of some becomes clear: that setting was only taken as the starting point for the proof because it necessitates contingency (الإمكان) — *the state of being neither necessary nor impossible in itself, requiring a cause for existence* — and every contingent being is needy, and the chain of its need must stop at a Necessary Being (واجب الوجود). Likewise, what is in the saying of others: that setting only negates Lordship from that which is described by it because it is motion (حركة), and every motion must have a mover, and it must ultimately end at a mover that is unmoved and stable, not changing in its essence, and that is Allah, exalted is His Name.

That is because while they are both demonstrative arguments, they negate Lordship from contingent beings and from moving things in the sense of the First Cause (العلية الأولى) to which all causes return, and the causality of origination and management at which all causes stop. The worshippers of the stars among the Sabians and others, although they held that every celestial body is sempiternal (قديم زماني), not subject to generation and corruption, and moving with a perpetual motion, they do not deny that all of them are caused due to their contingency, not independent in their existence nor in the effects of their existence from the Necessary Being, Mighty and Majestic.

Thus, the two arguments only stand against those who deny the existence of the Maker among the naturalists, not the Sabians and the worshippers of the lords of the species (أرباب الأنواع) and others among the pagans. Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was only arguing with the latter, not the former.

Furthermore, you have known that the argument was not constructed from the aspect of setting, but from the aspect of the absence of the attachment of love to something whose nature is setting and vanishing.

As for the one who refuted what they mentioned of formulating the argument from the aspect of contingency or motion by saying that interpreting setting as such is an interpretation of a thing by what is disparate from it—for the Arabs do not know setting in the sense of contingency or temporal origination (الحدوث), and likewise interpreting setting as change and motion is an error like the previous one—he missed that these people do not say that "setting" in the verse means contingency or motion. Rather, they say that setting was argued with because it necessitates contingency or motion and change. As for setting in the sense of absence after presence and hiddenness after manifestation, regardless of its necessitating contingency or change which are unsuitable for the station of the Necessary Being, it does not contradict Lordship, as this very objector admitted.

This is because the Necessary Being (الواجب تعالى), too, is absent from our senses, without His state undergoing any transition or change from presence to absence, or from manifestation to concealment. It is of no avail to claim that absence and concealment regarding Him (تعالى) are from our perspective, not His—due to our preoccupation with what distracts us from Him, rather than due to any limitation in His existence or deficiency in His dominion. For the absence of these celestial bodies, especially the sun due to the diurnal motion, is also from our perspective, insofar as we are parts of the earth that moves with its daily rotation. Thus, we are turned by it from facing and being aligned with these bodies to the opposite state; so we set from them in reality after our rising upon them, even if sensory error imagines otherwise for us.

Al-Razi sought to combine all these views, stating in his *Tafsir* the following: "Setting (الأفول) is an expression for the disappearance of a thing after its appearance. Once you know this, a questioner may ask: 'Setting only indicates temporal contingency (الحدوث) insofar as it is a movement (حركة). On this premise, rising (الطلوع) should also be an evidence for temporal contingency. Why then did Ibrahim (peace be upon him) abandon using their rising as evidence for their contingency, and instead relied on setting to prove this objective?'

The answer is: There is no doubt that rising and setting share in indicating temporal contingency. However, the evidence employed by prophets in the context of calling all creation to Allah must be manifest and clear, such that the intelligent, the simple, and the rational all share in understanding it. While the indication of movement (الحركة) toward temporal contingency is certain, it is subtle (دقيقة) and known only to the elite among creation. As for the indication of setting, it is a manifest indication known to everyone, for a star's dominion vanishes at the time of setting; thus, the indication of setting for this purpose was more complete.

Furthermore, some researchers have said: 'Descending into the realm of possibility (الإمكان) is itself a setting.' The best speech is that which contains a portion for the elite (الخواص), a portion for the middle class (الأوساط), and a portion for the common folk (العوام). The elite understand from 'setting' the concept of possibility (الإمكان) — *the state of a thing whose existence and non-existence are equally possible and which requires a cause to exist*; and every possible being is needy, never having its need terminated. Thus, one must ultimately reach Him who is transcendent above possibility, so that all needs are terminated by His existence, as He said: **وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى** — 'and that the terminus is toward your Lord' (53:42).

As for the middle class, they understand from 'setting' absolute movement; every moving thing is temporally contingent (محدث), and every contingent thing is in need of the Eternal (القديم), the All-Powerful. Thus, the setting one cannot be a god; rather, the God is He whom that setting one needs.

As for the common folk, they understand from 'setting' the act of sinking below the horizon. they witness that every star approaching its setting and sinking loses its light, its brightness diminishes, its dominion departs, and it becomes as if deposed. One who is such is not fit for divinity. Thus, this single phrase—namely His saying: **لا أُحِبُّ الْآفِلِينَ** (I do not love those that set)—is a word encompassing the share of the Near Ones (المقربين), the Companions of the Right (أصحاب اليمين), and the Companions of the Left (أصحاب الشمال); thus it was the most perfect of evidences and the most excellent of proofs.

There is another subtle point: he (peace be upon him) was debating them while they were astrologers. The doctrine of the practitioners of astrology is that when a star is in the eastern quadrant and ascending toward the midheaven, it is strong and of great influence. However, when it is western and near setting, it is weak in influence and of little power. Thus, he alerted them through this subtlety that the God is He whose power does not change into incapacity, nor His perfection into deficiency. Your doctrine is that the star, while in the western quadrant, is weak in power, deficient in influence, and incapable of governance (التدبير); this indicates a flaw in its divinity. Thus, it appears according to the view of the astrologers that setting has an additional specialty in being a cause for impugning its divinity." This concludes the relevant portion of his lengthy discourse.

By reflecting on what has preceded, you will realize that the artistry he displayed in dividing the proof into different portions according to the variation of souls, and his establishing it through various modes, is neither indicated by the wording of the verse, nor does it repel the doubt of the Sabians and the astrologers. For they do not view the celestial body as a God who is the Necessary Being (واجب الوجود) — *the self-existent Being whose non-existence is impossible* — of infinite power and absolute strength. Rather, they view it as a possible (ممكن), caused (معلول) being possessed of perpetual motion, which governs through its movement what is below it in the terrestrial world. None of the modes he mentioned repels this theory. It seems he became aware of this problem after his aforementioned remarks, so he expanded his discourse and spoke at length to escape the difficulty with arguments that avail nothing.

Furthermore, the second argument according to his formulation is an incomplete proof. For movement only indicates the temporal contingency of the moving object in terms of its attribute—which is the act of moving—not in terms of its essence (ذات). The full discussion of this is deferred to its proper place, and the recourse is to what we previously established in formulating the argument from the perspective of movement.

**Fourthly:** Ibrahim (peace be upon him) only presented these arguments according to what Allah, the Glorified, was showing him of the kingdom (ملكوت) — *the inner, celestial reality and sovereign dominion* — of the heavens and the earth, and according to what the course of his debate with his father and his people allowed, taking hold of the perceived and witnessed. This was either because he had not yet seen the details of the daily celestial and terrestrial events, as previously alluded to, or because he wished to debate them using what was within their sensory perception. Thus, he presented his saying: **هذا رَبِّي** (This is my Lord) when he witnessed one of the three bodies shining and rising, and he presented his saying: **لا أُحِبُّ الْآفِلِينَ** (I do not love those that set), or its equivalent, when he witnessed their setting.

By this, the answer becomes clear to what might be said: that His saying: **فَلَمَّا جَنَّ عَلَيْهِ اللَّيْلُ رَأى كَوْكَباً** — 'When night darkened over him, he saw a star' (6:76) — indicates that he (peace be upon him) had been with his people during the day immediately preceding that night; so why did he not mention the sun to negate its lordship?

For it is possible that he went out to his people for the debate and the time did not allow for more than what he debated with his father and his people regarding the matter of the idols. Thus, he continued debating them throughout the day or for the duration of the day he spent with them, until when he finished the debate, it was not long before the night darkened over him. There are other possibilities, such as clouds in the air, or his people being present for prayers and offerings only at the first rising, and he intended to confront them with what he was delivering to them.

**Fifthly:** The verses, as has been said, indicate that guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — is from Allah, the Glorified. As for misguidance (الإضلال), it is not attributed to Him (تعالى) in these verses. Rather, his saying: **لَئِنْ لَمْ يَهْدِنِي رَبِّي لَأَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الضَّالِّينَ** — 'If my Lord does not guide me, I will surely be among the people who are astray' (6:77) — provides some indication that it is the nature of man, according to the deficiency of his own soul, to be characterized by misguidance if his Lord does not guide him. This is what is gathered from the saying of the Exalted: **وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ ما زَكى مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَداً** — 'Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, not one of you would ever be pure' (24:21), and His saying: **إِنَّكَ لا تَهْدِي مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلكِنَّ اللهِ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ** — 'You cannot guide whomever you wish, but it is Allah who guides whomever He wishes' (28:56), among other verses.

Yes, there are verses that attribute misguidance to Him (تعالى), but examples like His saying: **وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** — 'and He misleads by it only the transgressors' (2:26) — clarify that the misguidance attributed to Him (تعالى) is the misguidance occurring as a recompense (المجازاة), not an initial misguidance (الإضلال الابتدائي). The discussion on the interpretation of this verse has already preceded in the first volume of this book.

**Sixthly:** In his argument to invalidate the lordship of the three bodies, he (peace be upon him) took the premise that he does not love the setting one because of its setting—which is for a human to lose it after finding it. This is the attribute to which the love that justifies worship (العبادة) does not attach. Since this was a consistent attribute in all physical entities (الجسمانيات) that journey toward vanishing, loss, destruction, and extinction, the argument was decisive against all forms of polytheism (الشرك) and idolatry (الوثنية). This even applies to what appears from some idolaters regarding the divinity of the "Lords of the Species" (أرباب الأنواع) and the luminous beings whose existence they acknowledge and claim are above matter and nature, transcendent beyond corporeality and movement. For they explicitly state that, despite the purity of their essence and the nobility of their existence, they are consumed before the Subsistent Light (النور القيومي) and humbled under the Subduing Oneness (القهر الأحدي). Since this is the attribute of what they claim, if love were to be directed toward them, it would only attach to the One who governs their affairs and rectifies their state, not to them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنِّي وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ** — 'Indeed I have turned my face toward Him who originated the heavens and the earth' (6:79) to the end of the verse.

Al-Raghib mentioned in *al-Mufradat*: "The origin of **origination** (الفطر) is splitting lengthwise. It is said: 'So-and-so split (فطر) such a thing,' and 'it split' (انفطر). He said: **هَلْ تَرى مِنْ فُطُورٍ** — 'Do you see any flaw?' (67:3), meaning a disruption or weakness therein. This may be by way of corruption or by way of rectification. He said: **السماء منفطر به** — 'the sky will be rent apart thereby' (73:18).

And 'I milked (فطرت) the sheep' using two fingers. And 'I kneaded (فطرت) the dough' when you knead it and bake it immediately. From this comes the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being*. Allah's 'origination' (فطر) of creation is His bringing the thing into existence and His innovating it (إبداعه) upon a form predisposed for a particular action. Thus, His saying: **فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها** — 'the nature of Allah on which He originated mankind' (30:30) — is an allusion from Him (تعالى) to what He originated—that is, innovated and embedded in people—of the knowledge of Him (تعالى). The primordial nature (فطرة) of Allah is what He embedded in man of his power to recognize faith, which is alluded to by His saying: **وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ** — 'If you ask them, ‘‘Who created them?’’ they will surely say, ‘‘Allah’’' (43:87)." End quote. He also mentioned: "**Inclination** (الحنف) is a turning away from misguidance toward uprightness, while **deviation** (الجنف) is a turning away from uprightness toward misguidance. He said: The Arabs called everyone who performed the pilgrimage or was circumcised a **hanif** (حنيفاً), to indicate that he followed the religion of Ibrahim (peace be upon him). The **ahnaf** (الأحنف) is one whose foot has a tilt; it is said he was named so out of optimism, or that it was borrowed for pure inclination." End quote.

When he (peace be upon him) disassociated himself from their polytheism and their partners by his saying: **يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي بَرِيءٌ** (O my people, indeed I am quit...), having approached this gradually by showing the lack of his heart's attachment to the partner when he said: **لا أُحِبُّ الْآفِلِينَ** (I do not love those that set), then hinting that the worship of a partner is misguidance when he said: **لَئِنْ لَمْ يَهْدِنِي رَبِّي لَأَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الضَّالِّينَ** (If my Lord does not guide me...), then the explicit disassociation from that by his saying: **يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ** (O my people, indeed I am quit of what you associate [with Allah]), he returned to his perfect monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — in Lordship (الربوبية). This consists of establishing Lordship and the right to be worshipped for the One who originated the heavens and the earth, and negating polytheism from himself, saying: **إِنِّي وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ حَنِيفاً** (Indeed I have turned my face toward Him who originated the heavens and the earth, as a hanif).

"Turning the face" is a metaphor for turning toward Allah, the Glorified, in worship (العبادة). For the necessary implication of servanthood (العبودية) and being a subject of Lordship (المربوبية) is that the subject servant attaches himself to his Lord in his power and will, calling upon Him and returning to Him in all his actions. There is no calling or returning except by turning the face and approaching Him; thus, he used "turning the face" as a metaphor for worship, which is calling and returning.

He mentioned his Lord—who is Allah, the Glorified, toward whom he turned his face—by His attribute that uniquely identifies Him without dispute, which is the origination of the heavens and the earth. He used the relative pronoun and its clause (الموصول والصلة) to indicate a specific referent (العهد) so that the matter would not be confused for any of them, saying: **لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ** (toward Him who originated the heavens and the earth). That is: I have approached with my worship the One to whom the bringing into existence and innovation of everything terminates, and He is the One whom he and they establish above all.

Then he negated everything else they called a partner by his saying: **حَنِيفاً** (as a hanif), meaning inclining toward Him away from others, negating any partner for Him. He emphasized this by his saying: **وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** (and I am not one of the polytheists). Thus, the sum of his saying: **إِنِّي وَجَّهْتُ** (Indeed I have turned...) etc., yielded the establishment of the right to be worshipped for Allah (تعالى) and the negation of any partner for Him, close to what the "Good Word" (الكلمة الطيبة) — **لا إِلهَ إِلَّا اللهُ** (There is no god but Allah) — yields.

The preposition *lam* in His saying: **لِلَّذِي** (toward Him who) is for the ultimate goal (للغاية) and conveys the meaning of "to" (إلى). Frequently, the *lam* is used for the goal just as "to" is used. He said: **أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُ لِلَّهِ** — 'submits his will to Allah' (2:112) and **وَمَنْ يُسْلِمْ وَجْهَهُ إِلَى اللهِ** — 'Whoever submits his will to Allah' (31:22).

In specifying the "origination of the heavens and the earth" from among His unique attributes (تعالى), and likewise from among the terms indicating creation such as the Maker (الباري), the Creator (الخالق), and the Innovator (البديع), there is an allusion to what Ibrahim (peace be upon him) preferred of the religion of the primordial nature (دين الفطرة). The description of this religion has been repeated in the Noble Quran as being the *hanif* religion of Ibrahim and the religion of the primordial nature—that is, the religion whose knowledge and laws are built upon the creation of man and the type of his existence, which accepts neither substitution nor change. For religion is the traversed path intended for reaching true happiness (السعادة الحقيقية). True happiness is the sought-after goal that a thing seeks according to the composition of its existence and its equipment with the means of perfection, as an external and real seeking. Far be it that man, or any other thing in creation, should find happiness in a matter for which he was not prepared according to his creation, or was prepared for its opposite—such as finding happiness in abandoning nourishment, marriage, or social interaction when he has been equipped for them; or finding happiness in flying like a bird or living in the depths of the seas like a fish when he has not been equipped with what suits that.

Thus, the true religion is that which accords in its laws with the primordial nature (الفطرة). Far be it from the threshold of Lordship to guide man—or any other creature charged with religion, if such exists—to a happy, felicitous end while it does not accord with the creation or he has not been equipped with that by which he may journey toward it. Indeed, the religion with Allah is Islam (الإسلام) — *the total submission to Allah's will* — which is submission to Allah according to what His handiwork and His bringing into existence guide to and indicate.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَحاجَّهُ قَوْمُهُ قالَ أَتُحاجُّونِّي فِي اللهِ وَقَدْ هَدانِ** — 'His people argued with him. He said, ‘‘Do you argue with me concerning Allah, while He has guided me?’’' (6:80). Allah (تعالى) divided his arguments (peace be upon him) into two types: one which he initiated and thus argued with the people, and the second which the people initiated and addressed him with after he disassociated himself from their gods. This which is addressed in the verse and what follows it is the second type.

Allah (تعالى) did not mention the argument they brought against him, but He alluded to it by His saying, narrating from Ibrahim (peace be upon him): **وَلا أَخافُ ما تُشْرِكُونَ بِهِ** (and I do not fear what you associate with Him). This is the argument for the necessity of worshipping their gods from the perspective of fear. It has preceded, and will come again, that what prompted them to take gods and worship them was one of two things: fear of their wrath and subdual due to the authority they possess over the events of the terrestrial world, or hope for blessing and happiness from them. The more influential of the two matters on their souls was the first—namely, fear. This is because people, by nature, view the blessings and material happiness in their hands as their own possession, either as a result of their effort in the way of livelihood in acquiring wealth and gaining status and prestige, or from what great fortune or happy luck has granted them—such as one who inherits wealth from a predecessor, happens upon a treasure and possesses it, or leads his people through his father's leadership.

Thus, the path of hope is of little influence in the necessity of servitude (العبودية), to the extent that Muslims—despite the complete divine teaching in their hands—are less affected by promise and glad tidings than they are by threat and warning. For this very reason, we see that the Quran mentions warning (الإنذار) as a function of the prophets more than it mentions giving glad tidings (التبشير), though both are among their functions and the methods they employ in the religious call.

In short, the people of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) chose the path of fear in their argument with him when they spoke to him regarding the matter of the gods. They intimidated him with the subdual and wrath of the gods and exhorted him to follow their path and adhere to their way in seeking proximity through the gods, and to reject the claim of the Lordship of Allah, the Glorified, establishing Him only in the position they established Him in—which is that He is merely the One to whom everything ultimately terminates.

When he (peace be upon him) found their speech resolving into two parts—deterrence from the claim of Allah's Lordship and incitement toward the claim of their gods' Lordship—he argued against them from both sides, though the first side is not independent of the second, as will follow.

What he presented in arguing against their dispute concerning Allah, the Glorified, is his saying: **أَتُحاجُّونِّي فِي اللهِ وَقَدْ هَدانِ** (Do you argue with me concerning Allah, while He has guided me?). That is: I am situated in a matter already settled and am guided by the guidance of my Lord, as He has granted me knowledge through what He showed me of the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and inspired me thereby with an argument by which I negate the lordship of others among idols and stars. I am not independent of a Lord who governs my affairs, which led me to the conclusion that He alone is the Lord, without partner. Since He has guided me to Him, I have no need to listen to your argument or to search for Lordship a second time; for searching only benefits the seeker, and there is no seeking after reaching the goal.

This is what the literal sense of the verse provides by immediate intuition. However, there is a subtler meaning that appears through reflection: his saying: **وَقَدْ هَدانِ** (while He has guided me) is an inference using the guidance itself, not a declaration of independence from inference due to guidance. Its formulation is that Allah guided me through what He taught me of the argument for negating the lordship of others and establishing His Lordship; and the very fact of His guidance is a proof that He is the Lord and there is no Lord besides Him. For guidance to the Lord is part of governance (التدبير), and thus it is the affair of one who is the Lord. Had Allah, the Glorified, not been my Lord, He would not have guided me, and He would have assigned that guidance to the one who is the Lord; but Allah is the one who guided me, so He is my Lord.

They could not say: "The one who taught you what you know and inspired you with the argument is one of our gods," because a thing does not guide to what harms it, kills its mention, and ruins its affair. Thus, his (peace be upon him) being guided to the negation of their lordship cannot correctly be attributed to them.

However, they could have said—or they did say: "That is the act of one of our gods; he did that to you by way of subdual and wrath, distancing you from the claim of their lordship and prompting you with these arguments when he found corruption in your view and a malady in your soul." This is similar to what the people of 'Ad said to Hud (peace be upon him) when he called them to the monotheism of Allah, the Glorified, and argued with them that Allah is the one who must be hoped for and feared, and that their gods neither benefit nor harm. They replied to him that some of our gods have afflicted you with evil. The Exalted said in their story, narrating from Hud (peace be upon him): **وَيا قَوْمِ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً وَيَزِدْكُمْ قُوَّةً إِلى قُوَّتِكُمْ وَلا تَتَوَلَّوْا مُجْرِمِينَ ، قالُوا يا هُودُ** — until He said — **إِنْ نَقُولُ إِلَّا اعْتَراكَ بَعْضُ آلِهَتِنا بِسُوءٍ قالَ إِنِّي أُشْهِدُ اللهَ وَاشْهَدُوا أَنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ ، مِنْ دُونِهِ فَكِيدُونِي جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ لا تُنْظِرُونِ** — '"O my people! Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently: He will send copious rains for you from the sky, and add power to your power. Do not turn your backs [on Him] as guilty ones." They said, "O Hud!...' [until He said] 'All we say is that some of our gods have visited you with some evil." He said, "I call Allah to witness, and you [too] bear witness, that I am quit of what you associate [with Him] besides Him. So scheme against me, all of you, then do not give me any respite."' (11:52-55).

Thus, his (peace be upon him) saying: **وَلا أَخافُ ما تُشْرِكُونَ بِهِ** (and I do not fear what you associate with Him) etc., negates this doubt. Just as it negates this doubt, it is a complete proof negating the lordship of their partners.

Its summary is: You call me to the claim of the lordship of your partners and to reject the claim of the Lordship of my Lord by frightening me that your partners might touch me with evil, and you intimidate me by casting doubt upon that by which I have been guided. But I do not fear what you associate with Him, because they are all governed creatures (مخلوقات مدبرة) that possess neither benefit nor harm. Since I do not fear them, your argument falls and your doubt is removed.

And had I feared them, the fear occurring in my soul would not have been the handiwork of your partners, for they are not capable of anything. Rather, it would have been the handiwork of my Lord; He would have been the one who willed that I fear your partners, so I feared them. Thus, this fear would be another proof of His Lordship and another sign among the signs of His monotheism, necessitating the sincerity of worship to Him, not a proof for the lordship of your partners or an argument necessitating their worship.

The proof that this is from my Lord is that He encompasses everything in knowledge (وسع كل شيء علما); thus He knows everything that occurs and transpires of good and evil in His kingdom, which He brought into existence for sound and precise purposes. How could He know of something in His kingdom that benefits or harms and remain silent, not meeting it with one of two things: either prevention or permission?

So if any fear were to occur in my soul, it would be by a will (مشية) from Allah and permission, according to what befits His holy threshold. That would be part of the governance (التدبير) indicating His Lordship and negating the lordship of others. Will you not then remember and return to what you perceive with your intellects and what your primordial nature (فطرة) guides you to?

This is one way of formulating the argument contained in His saying: **وَلا أَخافُ ما تُشْرِكُونَ بِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ رَبِّي شَيْئاً وَسِعَ رَبِّي كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْماً أَفَلا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ** (and I do not fear what you associate with Him, unless my Lord wishes something. My Lord embraces all things in His knowledge. Will you not then take admonition?). Accordingly, his saying: **وَلا أَخافُ ما تُشْرِكُونَ بِهِ** (and I do not fear what you associate with Him) is like a completion of the argument in his saying: **أَتُحاجُّونِّي فِي اللهِ وَقَدْ هَدانِ** (Do you argue with me concerning Allah, while He has guided me?). It is, at the same time, a complete proof in itself for invalidating the lordship of their partners by the absence of fear of them. His saying: **إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ رَبِّي شَيْئاً** (unless my Lord wishes something) is like a discourse in the argument on the premise of concession—that is: you argue for the necessity of their worship through fear, yet there is no fear in my soul; and if fear were assumed, it would be a proof for the Lordship of my Lord, not for the lordship of your partners, for it would be from a will of my Lord. His saying: **وَسِعَ رَبِّي كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْماً** (My Lord embraces all things in His knowledge) is an explanation and justification for the assumed fear being attributed to the will of his Lord; for the Originator of the heavens and the earth is not ignorant of what occurs in His kingdom, so nothing occurs except by permission from Him. Thus, He is the one who governs its affair and maintains its Lordship. His saying: **أَفَلا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ** (Will you not then take admonition?) is a reproachful interrogation and an indication that the argument is primordial (فطرية). This is one view.

The commentators have several opinions regarding the verse:

As for His saying (تعالى): **قالَ أَتُحاجُّونِّي فِي اللهِ وَقَدْ هَدانِ** (He said, ‘‘Do you argue with me concerning Allah, while He has guided me?’’), most of them have presented the first of the two views we previously mentioned. Its summary is that he repels their objection to his monotheism by stating that he is independent of arguing over it, for Allah has guided him, and with guidance there is no need for argument. However, the apparent context is that he is in the position of arguing, and its implication is that his speech is an argument for monotheism, not a declaration of independence from argument.

As for His saying (تعالى): **لا أَخافُ ما تُشْرِكُونَ بِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ رَبِّي شَيْئاً** (and I do not fear what you associate with Him, unless my Lord wishes something), they mentioned regarding the first part something close to what we presented. As for the exception, it was said: its meaning is "unless my Lord overcomes these idols with which you frighten me, so He brings them to life and empowers them so they harm and benefit; then their harm and benefit would at that time be a proof of their temporal contingency and of the monotheism of Allah, the Glorified." In other words: the meaning is that I do not fear them in any state, unless my Lord wishes that these partners come to life and thus harm and benefit, so I would fear them; and at that time, Lordship would be for Allah and the contingency of your partners would be manifest.

This view, although close to what we presented in one respect, the attribution of benefit and harm to the partners if they were alive—given that some of them are alive according to them, such as the angels and the "Lords of the Species," and some benefit and harm according to the apparent relation, such as the sun—contradicts the divine teaching in His book. For the Quran explicitly states that none possesses benefit or harm except Allah, the Glorified.

Likewise, what was mentioned regarding this being an indication of the contingency of your partners is a matter that does not harm the idolaters. For they, as you have known, do not deny that the idols and their lords are caused (معلولة) by Allah and created by Him. The claim of temporal pre-eternity (القدم الزماني) for some of them does not negate their possibility (إمكانها) or their being caused according to them.

It was also said: the meaning of the exception is "I do not fear your partners, and I except from the generality of fear at all times that my Lord should wish to punish me for some of my sins or afflict me with a misfortune initially." In other words, the sentence is an exception from a meaning more general than what the preceding sentence indicates. His saying: **وَلا أَخافُ** (and I do not fear) etc., indicated the negation of fear of their partners, and his saying: **إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ** (unless [He] wishes) etc., is an exception from every fear. The estimation would be: I do not fear what you associate with Him, nor anything else, except that my Lord should wish something I dislike, either initially or as a recompense, for then I would fear it. The aspect of artificiality in this meaning needs no explanation.

As for His saying: **وَسِعَ رَبِّي كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْماً** (My Lord embraces all things in His knowledge), it was said: it is a praise from him (peace be upon him) for his Lord after completing the argument.

It was also said: it is an insinuation against their idols, since they know nothing and perceive nothing. To this it is objected that an insinuation regarding something like power (القدرة) is closer to the requirements of the context than an insinuation regarding knowledge; so what is the reason for turning from power to knowledge? The problem also applies to the previous view.

It was also said: when he excepted what his Lord wishes of the misfortunes that might befall him, he explained by his saying: **وَسِعَ رَبِّي كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْماً** (My Lord embraces all things in His knowledge) that He (تعالى) is the Knower of the Unseen, so He does nothing except what is right, good, and wise. To this it is objected that it would be more appropriate then to mention wisdom (الحكمة) in place of knowledge, or at least to mention wisdom along with knowledge, as in most instances.

It was also said: it is like a justification for the exception, due to the possibility that it has already preceded in the knowledge of Allah (تعالى) that he be afflicted with an evil of which the idols are the cause—such as if He wishes that an idol fall upon him and wound him, or the heat of the sun affect him and make him ill or kill him. To this it is objected that resorting to power or wisdom is more appropriate for justification than knowledge.

It was also said: its meaning is that the knowledge of my Lord has embraced everything and encompassed it, and His will (مشيئته) is linked to His encompassing, eternal knowledge, and His power executes His will. Thus, it is impossible for any of the creatures you worship, or any others, to have any influence whatsoever on His attributes or on His acts proceeding from them, whether through intercession (شفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — or otherwise. That would only be the case if the knowledge of Allah (تعalte) did not encompass everything, such that the intercessors and intermediaries would inform Him of aspects of the preponderance of action or omission through intercession or otherwise which He did not know; then that would be what carries Him toward harm or benefit, or giving or withholding.

This speaker said: "We have taken this meaning for this sentence from the arguments of Allah (تعالى) for negating the polytheistic intercession, such as His saying: **مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ يَعْلَمُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَما خَلْفَهُمْ وَلا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْءٍ مِنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِما شاءَ** — 'Who is it that may intercede with Him except with His permission? He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes' (2:255)." He said: "This is the most preponderant of views, and it is of the type of interpreting the Quran by the Quran." This is a summary of his words.

Its summary is that His saying: **وَسِعَ رَبِّي كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْماً** (My Lord embraces all things in His knowledge) is an explanation and justification for the generality of the negation of fear of the gods and others. It is as if he said: I do not fear the harm of anything among your gods or other creatures, for my Lord knows everything, so He completes it by His will and executes it by His power; thus He has no need for an intercessor to inform Him of what He was ignorant of, such that it would have an influence on the acts of Allah (تعالى) and intercession.

You know that negating this influence, just as it requires the vastness of His knowledge (تعالى), likewise requires the absolute nature of power and will—and the will, moreover, is an attribute of act (صفة فعل), not of essence (ذات), as the speaker assumes. So what benefit is the vastness of knowledge if His power and will were not absolute? The witness for this is his own speech in which he formulated the view using knowledge, will, and power all together.

In short, this view is not completed by the vastness of knowledge alone, but is completed by it and by the absolute nature of power and will; yet only the vastness of knowledge was mentioned in the verse.

As for what he mentioned regarding the indication of the verses of intercession for that, the mentioned verses are presented to establish intercession in the sense of mediation in causality (السببية) by permission from Allah, the Glorified, not that they negate it as he imagined, claiming that he interprets the Quran by the Quran. How could it be otherwise? The desire for the removal of causes (الأسباب) from the witnessed world is a desire for what cannot be desired. The Noble Quran, from its beginning to its end, speaks of causality and builds upon the general principle of cause and effect (العلية والمعلولية). The discussion on these meanings has preceded repeatedly in the previous volumes of the book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَيْفَ أَخافُ ما أَشْرَكْتُمْ وَلا تَخافُونَ أَنَّكُمْ أَشْرَكْتُمْ بِاللهِ ما لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطاناً** — 'How should I fear what you associate [with Him], while you do not fear that you have associated with Allah that for which He has not sent down any authority to you?' (6:81) etc. Then he (peace be upon him) turned back upon them with another argument proving the contradiction between their word and their deed. In other words: their state belies their statement. Its summary is that you command me to fear that which should not be feared, while you yourselves do not fear Him who must be feared; so I am more entitled to security than you if I disobey you and do not follow your command.

As for the fact that what you command me to fear should not be feared, it is because there is no evidence for the idols and their lords being independent in harm and benefit such that they necessitate fear of them. As for your not fearing Him who must be feared, it is because you yourselves have established partners for Allah, the Glorified, in Lordship, yet Allah has not sent down to you any proof (برهاناً) for that upon which one can rely. For the making and bringing into existence belong to Allah, the Glorified; thus to Him belongs the kingdom and to Him belongs the judgment. If He had taken some of His creatures as a partner for Himself, thereby necessitating for us the worship of His partner, it would be for Him, not for anyone else, to clarify that to us and uncover the face of reality therein. The way to do that is to accompany it with signs and verses indicating that He has a partnership in such-and-forth; and that is either through revelation (وحي) or a proof (برهان) that relies on external effects. None of that exists.

According to this formulation, the saying of the Exalted: **ما أَشْرَكْتُمْ** (what you associate) is restricted, according to what is gathered from the context, by what His saying: **أَشْرَكْتُمْ بِاللهِ ما لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطاناً** (you have associated with Allah that for which He has not sent down any authority to you) is restricted by. He only mentioned this restriction when mentioning their lack of fear of their polytheism because the argument is more in need of its mention there, which is manifest. His saying: **فَأَيُّ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ أَحَقُّ بِالْأَمْنِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** (So which of the two parties has more right to security, if you know?) is part of the completion of the argument. The whole is a proof of their self-contradiction in calling him (peace be upon him) to fear their gods; for they command him to fear in a situation where it is not necessary, while they themselves do not fear in a situation where it is necessary.

By the previous explanation, it appears that describing their partners as being those for whom Allah has not sent down any authority (سلطاناً) is a hypothetical assumption (افتراض) required by the type of argument placed in the discourse. It has no counter-implication (مفهوم) that would establish the possibility of Allah commanding the taking of partners as gods to be worshipped. It is equivalent to our saying: "You have no evidence for what you claimed," in response to one who frightens us with a mythical subject he claims might benefit or harm. We may replace our saying that—if we wished to speak with the tongue of monotheism—with our saying: "Allah has sent down no evidence for that." The discourse, according to logical analysis, resolves into a hypothetical syllogism (قياس استثنائي) in which the contradiction of the antecedent in the conditional is excepted to produce the contradiction of the consequent, similar to our saying:

"If Allah had sent down to you an authority indicating their power to harm, your taking of partners out of fear of them would be in its place; but He has not sent down an authority, so your taking of partners is not in its place." It is well known that there is no counter-implication in this syllogism; thus there is no need to say that the restriction by His saying: **لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطاناً** (He has not sent down any authority for it) is for sarcasm, or to indicate that this is an inseparable attribute of their partners, similar to the saying of the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَدْعُ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ لا بُرْهانَ لَهُ بِهِ** — 'Whoever invokes another god besides Allah, for which he has no proof' (23:117), among other interpretations.

The preposition *ba* in His saying: **لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ** (He has not sent down with it) is for accompaniment (للمعية) or causality (السببية). He (peace be upon him) used the metaphor of "the two parties" (الفريقين) for them and himself, and did not say: "I and you" or similar, so as to be further from provoking zealotry and inciting tribalism, as has been said, and to indicate their separation and the schism between them due to the difference in the "Principle of Principles" and the mother of true knowledge, such that they would not harmonize in anything thereafter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ** — 'Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be security, and they are the [rightly] guided' (6:82). He asked them in the previous verse, within what he established of the argument, about who is more entitled to security, saying: **فَأَيُّ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ أَحَقُّ بِالْأَمْنِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** (So which of the two parties has more right to security, if you know?). Then he answered them regarding what he asked them, because the answer is clear and the two disputing parties do not differ over it. An answer of this nature may be initiated by the questioner without waiting for the questioned; for the questioned will not oppose the questioner in that such that he would fear rejection. Allah (تعالى) has narrated their admission of that in the story of the breaking of the idols: **قالَ بَلْ فَعَلَهُ كَبِيرُهُمْ هذا فَسْئَلُوهُمْ إِنْ كانُوا يَنْطِقُونَ ، فَرَجَعُوا إِلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ فَقالُوا إِنَّكُمْ أَنْتُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ ، ثُمَّ نُكِسُوا عَلى رُؤُسِهِمْ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ ما هؤُلاءِ يَنْطِقُونَ** — 'He said, "Rather it was this, their biggest one, who did it. Ask them, if they can speak." Thereat they came to themselves and said [to one another], "Indeed it is you who are the wrongdoers." Then they relapsed into their [former] state: "You already know that they do not speak."' (21:63-65).

This is what the context of the speech requires: that the verse be from the speech of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and a statement of his. As for its being from the speech of his people and an answer narrated from them, or likewise its being from Allah, the Glorified, by way of judging between the two disputing parties, these are views that the context does not support at all.

In any case, the speech contains strong emphasis through multiple attributions in nominal sentences (جمل اسمية). That which is in His saying: **لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ** (for them is security) is a nominal sentence which is the predicate (خبر) for His saying: **أُولئِكَ** (those). The whole is a nominal sentence which is the predicate for His saying: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (those who believe) etc. The whole is a nominal sentence. Likewise, what is coordinated with His saying: **لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ** of His saying: **وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ** (and they are guided). This yields that there is no doubt in the exclusivity of security and guidance for those who believe and do not cover their faith with wrongdoing (ظلم), without any uncertainty.

There is no doubt that the verse indicates that the specialty of security and guidance is among the effects of faith (الإيمان), conditioned upon its not being tainted with wrongdoing (ظلم). **Tainting** (اللبس) is covering (الستر), as Al-Raghib mentioned in *al-Mufradat*.
