The origin of *labs*—with a *fatha* on the *lam*—is covering; it is a metaphor intended to indicate that this wrongdoing does not invalidate the origin of faith, for it is primordial (فطري) and does not accept total invalidation. Rather, it covers it and corrupts its effect, not allowing it to produce its correct effect.

**Wrongdoing** (الظلم) — *the act of departing from the center of justice* — although it is an indefinite noun (نكرة) occurring in the context of negation in the verse, which necessitates generality and the absence of faith being joined with anything that is truly called wrongdoing at all, the context indicates that wrongdoing is a barrier to the appearance of faith and its manifestation with its desired good effects. This was a contextual clue (قرينة) that the intended wrongdoing is the type of wrongdoing that exerts an evil influence on faith, rather than the wrongdoing that has no effect on it.

This is because wrongdoing, although it is suspected that the first meaning people transitioned to was social wrongdoing—which is transgression against a social right by stripping security from the person, honor, or property of one of the members of society without a justifying right—people later expanded it. They called every violation of a law or an established custom "wrongdoing," and indeed every sin and disobedience to a masterly command "wrongdoing" from the sinner toward himself. Indeed, disobedience to Allah, the Glorified, because of His established right to obedience, is wrongdoing. Even violating a duty is wrongdoing, even if it is out of oversight, forgetfulness, or ignorance, even if they do not build upon holding this violator accountable and punishing him for what he did. Rather, they consider one who violates advice or an instructional command—even if the matter was confused for him and he erred in his violation without intent—as a wrongdoer to himself. Even one who is negligent in observing medical health instructions or violates any of the factors influencing the health of his temperament, even if unintentionally, is considered a wrongdoer to himself, though it is wrongdoing without awareness. The criterion in all of that is the expansion in the meaning of wrongdoing through its analysis.

In short, wrongdoing has a wide range—as you have known—but not every individual instance of it exerts an evil influence on faith. For its categories that do not involve sin, disobedience, or masterly violation—such as when its occurrence is due to oversight, forgetfulness, ignorance, or when its occurrence was not perceived, for example—are all among those that do not influence the faith whose function is to bring one closer to true happiness and success and the attainment of the pleasure of the Lord, the Glorified. This is manifest; thus, the effect of faith producing its result is not conditioned upon the absence of any of that.

So His saying: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ** (Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be security) means the conditioning of faith in its granting of security from every sin and disobedience that corrupts its effect, by the absence of wrongdoing. However, there is a subtlety here: intentional sin—as we have fully discussed at the end of the sixth volume of the book—is a matter of different levels according to the variation of understandings. Some wrongdoing is an intentional disobedience for one group, while it is not so for others. For example, one standing at the crossroads of polytheism and monotheism is one who sees that the world has a Maker who originated its parts, split its regions, and held its earth and sky. He sees that he himself and others are created, subject to Lordship, and governed; and that true human life only finds happiness through faith in Him and submission to Him. The wrongdoing that appears to this person is polytheism (الشرك) toward Allah and believing in the lordship of others, such as idols, stars, and so forth, as Ibrahim (peace be upon him) establishes by his saying: **وَكَيْفَ أَخافُ ما أَشْرَكْتُمْ وَلا تَخافُونَ أَنَّكُمْ أَشْرَكْتُمْ بِاللهِ ما لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطاناً** (How should I fear what you associate [with Him], while you do not fear that you have associated with Allah that for which He has not sent down any authority to you?). Thus, the faith that exerts its effect for this person is only conditioned, in its granting of security from wretchedness, by his not tainting it with the wrongdoing of polytheism and its disobedience.

One who has traversed this stage and believed in Allah alone then faces, as wrongdoing, the major sins (الكبائر) among disobediences—such as undutifulness to parents, consuming the property of the orphan, killing a protected soul, adultery, and drinking wine. His faith, in its exerting its good effects, is conditioned by avoiding this type of wrongdoing. Allah has promised him to expiate the evil deeds and minor sins if he avoids the major ones of what he is forbidden. The Exalted said: **إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكِفرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ وَنُدْخِلْكُمْ مُدْخَلاً كَرِيماً** — 'If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you to a noble entrance' (4:31). The corruption of the effect of this faith is the wretchedness through the punishment for these sins, even if it is not an eternal punishment without end like the punishment for polytheism, but rather a finite one—either by the arrival of its term or through intercession and the like.

One who has taken this provision of godwariness (التقوى) and attained some knowledge of the station of his Lord is then responsible for types of wrongdoing that appear to him according to the degree of his knowledge of his Lord—such as performing disliked acts (المكروهات), abandoning recommended acts (المستحبات), and immersing oneself in permissible things (المباحات). Beyond that are sins in the level of noble character and divine traits, and behind that are the sins that obstruct the path of love and surround the carpet of proximity. Faith in each of these levels only secures the one characterized by it and repels wretchedness from him if it is free from the tainting of the wrongdoing appropriate to that level.

Thus, for the saying of the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ** (Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing), there is an absolute sense (إطلاق) in terms of wrongdoing, but it is an absolute sense that varies according to the variation of the levels of faith. Since the context is the position of debating the polytheists, the negated wrongdoing applies to the wrongdoing of polytheism specifically. The security that this faith grants is security from what is feared of eternal wretchedness and everlasting punishment. The verse, moreover, is an independent verse in terms of its exposition, regardless of the specificity of the case; it conveys that security and guidance only follow upon faith on the condition of the absence of all forms of wrongdoing that taint it and cover its effect, in the sense previously explained.

As for the faith mentioned in the verse, it contains an absolute sense. The intended meaning is faith in Lordship (الربوبية) which is capable of being restricted by what rectifies it or corrupts it. Then, when it is restricted by His saying: **وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ** (and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing), it becomes specified as faith in the Lordship of Allah, the Glorified, and the rejection of others among their partners. For Ibrahim (peace be upon him) mentioned in what the previous verse narrates from him that their claim of the lordship of their partners and their faith in them—given that they are among the creation of Allah—is a claim for which they have no evidence from Allah's side nor any authority. He mentioned that by their faith in their partners, they seek to avoid an evil and secure themselves from a wretchedness which the partners have no power to repel, for they neither harm nor benefit. As for him (peace be upon him), he feared and believed in the One who is his Originator, who is the one acting through guidance and the Governor who has a will and a wish in every matter due to the vastness of His knowledge. Then he asked them: which of the two parties has more right to security and is successful through faith in the Lord? For each of the two parties has a faith in a Lord, even if they differ regarding the Lord; and the one who believed among them is between one believing in a Lord for whose Lordship there is evidence, and one believing in a Lord for whose Lordship there is no evidence—rather, the evidence is to the contrary.

From here it appears that the intended meaning of faith in His saying: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (Those who believe) is absolute faith in Lordship. Then, by its restriction through His saying: **وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ** (and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing), it becomes specified as faith in Allah, the Glorified, which is the true faith. So understand.

It has become clear from what has preceded: First, that the intended meaning of faith is faith in Lordship, rather than faith in the existence of the Maker of the world, in contrast to those who deny His existence.

Second: That wrongdoing in the verse is the absolute sense of what harms faith and corrupts it among disobediences. Likewise, the intended meaning of security is absolute security from the wretchedness of disobediences and sins, and by guidance, the absolute deliverance from their misguidance, even if it applies according to the case to the disobedience of polytheism specifically.

Third: That the absolute sense of wrongdoing varies according to the variation of the levels of faith.

Some commentators said regarding the meaning of the generality of wrongdoing in the verse: "Security in the verse is restricted to those who believe and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing. If the generality therein is taken in its absolute sense without observing the subject of faith, the meaning would be: those who believe and do not mix their faith with any wrongdoing to themselves—neither in their faith, nor in their physical and psychological acts, whether religious or worldly—nor to others among creatures, whether rational beings or animals. For such there is security from the religious punishment of Allah (تعالى) for committing disobediences and abominations, and His worldly punishment for not observing its cause in the linking of causes to effects—such as poverty, maladies, and diseases—unlike others who have wronged themselves or others. For the wrongdoers have no security; rather, every wrongdoer is subject to punishment, even if Allah (تعالى), due to the vastness of His mercy, does not punish every wrongdoer for every wrongdoing, but rather pardons many sins of the world, and punishes whom He wills and forgives whom He wills in the Hereafter for what is less than polytheism with Him."

He said: "This meaning in the interpretation of the verse is correct in itself. It follows from it that absolute security from the fear of Allah's punishment—whether religious and worldly, or legislative and existential—is not valid for any of the legally responsible (المكلفين), let alone the fear of awe and majesty by which the people of perfection are distinguished."

He said: "As for the meaning of the verse on the assumption of the absence of absolute generality, it is that those who believe and do not mix their faith with a great wrongdoing—which is polytheism with Allah—for such there is security, unlike others, from the religious punishment related to the origin of religion, which is eternity in the Abode of Punishment; and in what is less than that, they are between fear and hope."

He said: "The apparent sense of the verse is generality." He argued for this using the understanding of the Companions according to what was narrated: that when the verse was revealed, it was difficult for the people, and they said: "O Messenger of Allah, which of us has not wronged himself?" So he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) informed them that the intended meaning is polytheism. Perhaps the context and the subject being faith also suggest this. This is a summary of his words.

Therein are points of objection: First, that what he argued for of generality using the understanding of the Companions is other than what he formulated of the meaning of generality. For what they understood of wrongdoing is what is equivalent to disobedience, while what he formulated is more general than that.

Second: That what he formulated of the generality of wrongdoing—even regarding instances of wrongdoing that are not part of disobedience at all—and then his judging the correctness of interpreting the verse by it, is foreign to the indication of the verse. For the verse is in the position of explaining that security and guidance are among the effects of faith, but on the condition that it is not joined by a wrongdoing that covers it and corrupts its effect. This wrongdoing is disobedience in some way. As for what is not considered disobedience—such as eating food harmful to the health of the body by mistake—it is well known that it does not corrupt the effect of faith in terms of security and guidance. The verse does not intend to explain the effects of wrongdoing, whatever they may be, even regardless of faith. For He (تعالى) said: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ** (Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing); thus He made faith the subject and restricted it by the absence of wrongdoing, and made its effect security and guidance. He did not make wrongdoing the subject such that the verse would be presented to explain its effects.

Thus, the verse was presented to explain the effects that follow upon correct faith. As for wrongdoing with its wide range and the effect following upon it, the verse does not address that at all. So his saying: "This meaning in the interpretation of the verse is correct in itself" is entirely invalid.

Third: That his saying: "It follows from it that absolute security is not valid for any of the legally responsible" is explicit in that the verse has no instance of application (مصداق) in view of the absolute sense he formulated. Its implication is the falling of the speech from utility. What utility is there in placing in an argument a statement that has no instance of application at all?

Fourthly: The view he chose regarding the meaning of the verse—that it refers to a specific type of wrongdoing (الظلم), namely polytheism (الشرك)—is not sound. While the verse, by virtue of the generality of its wording, indicates the necessity of faith (الإيمان) not being coupled with polytheism for it to produce its effect, this is a matter of applying a general term (اللفظ العام) to its specific instance (مورده الخاص). As for intending a specific meaning (المعنى الخاص) from a general term without any contextual evidence (قرينة)—whether situational (حالية) or verbal (مقالية), connected (متصلة) or detached (منفصلة)—this is something that the craft of eloquence (صناعة البلاغة) does not accept, as is evident.

As for what he pointed to regarding the saying of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "It is only polytheism (الشرك)," it is not explicit in stating that polytheism is the literal intended meaning (مراد لفظي) of the verse; rather, it is a case of application (الانطباق). The discussion regarding this tradition (الحديث) will follow in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions," Allah the Exalted willing.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَتِلْكَ حُجَّتُنا آتَيْناها إِبْراهِيمَ عَلى قَوْمِهِ نَرْفَعُ دَرَجاتٍ مَنْ نَشاءُ** — "This was Our argument, which We gave to Abraham against his people. We raise in rank whomever We wish." (6:83). The use of the demonstrative for the distant (تلك) to refer to the argument (الحجة) serves to glorify and magnify its status, as it was a decisive argument (حجة قاطعة) proceeding upon the path of the primordial nature (صراط الفطرة), with its premises derived therefrom.

As for His saying: **نَرْفَعُ دَرَجاتٍ مَنْ نَشاءُ** — "We raise in rank whomever We wish," the ranks (الدرجات) are—as has been said—the steps of a ladder. The term was then extended to be applied to the levels of spiritual perfection (مراتب الكمال), such as knowledge (العلم), faith (الإيمان), honor (الكرامة), and status (الجاه), among others. Thus, His raising of whom He wills among His servants in rank is His singling them out for spiritual perfections and true virtues, whether in acquired goods (الخيرات الكسبية) like knowledge and godwariness (التقوى), or non-acquired ones like prophethood (النبوة), messengership (الرسالة), provision (الرزق), and others.

The word "ranks" (الدرجات), being indefinite (نكرة) in an affirmative context (سياق الإيجاب), is non-specific (مهملة) rather than absolute (مطلقة). However, what is certain of its meaning, considering the specific context, is the ranks of knowledge and guidance (الهداية). Allah raised Abraham (عليه‌السلام) through His guidance, by showing him the kingdom (ملكوت) of the heavens and the earth, and by granting him certainty (اليقين) and the decisive argument—all of which pertain to knowledge. Indeed, the Exalted has said regarding the ranks of knowledge: **يَرْفَعِ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجاتٍ** — "Allah will raise those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge in rank." (58:11).

Then He concluded the verse with His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ** — "Indeed your Lord is all-wise, all-knowing," to affirm that all of that was by a wisdom (حكمة) and knowledge (علم) from Him, just as the arguments He gave to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) mentioned in the Surah before this argument were from His wisdom and knowledge. In the discourse, there is a transition (التفات) from the first person to the third person to comfort the heart of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and to solidify the insights mentioned therein.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Uyun*: Na'im ibn Abdillah ibn Tamim al-Qurashi (رضي الله عنه) narrated to us, saying: My father narrated to us from Hamdan ibn Sulayman al-Nayshaburi from Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm, who said: I attended the assembly of al-Ma'mun while al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) was with him. Al-Ma'mun said to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, is it not your view that the prophets are infallible (معصومون)?" He replied: "Yes." He then asked him about verses from the Quran concerning them. Among his questions was: "Tell me about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, regarding Abraham: **فَلَمَّا جَنَّ عَلَيْهِ اللَّيْلُ رَأى كَوْكَباً قالَ هذا رَبِّي** — 'When night darkened over him, he saw a star and said, "This is my lord."' (6:76)."

Imam al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) replied: "Abraham encountered three groups: a group that worshipped Venus, a group that worshipped the moon, and a group that worshipped the sun. This was when he emerged from the subterranean chamber (السرب) in which he had been hidden. When the night darkened over him and he saw Venus, he said: **هذا رَبِّي** (This is my lord) by way of denial (الإنكار) and inquiry (الاستخبار). When the star set, he said: **لا أُحِبُّ الْآفِلِينَ** (I do not like those who set), because setting (الأفول) is an attribute of the originated (المحدث), not an attribute of the Eternal (القديم). When he saw the moon rising, he said: **هذا رَبِّي** (This is my lord) by way of denial and inquiry. When it set, he said: **لَئِنْ لَمْ يَهْدِنِي رَبِّي لَأَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الضَّالِّينَ** — 'Had my Lord not guided me, I would surely have been among the people who are astray.' (6:77). When morning came and he saw the sun rising, he said: **هذا رَبِّي هذا أَكْبَرُ** (This is my lord; this is bigger) than Venus and the moon—by way of denial and inquiry, not by way of reporting (الإخبار) and affirmation (الإقرار). When it set, he said to the three groups—the worshippers of Venus, the moon, and the sun: **يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ إِنِّي وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ حَنِيفاً وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — 'O my people! I am indeed quit of what you associate [with Allah]. Indeed I have turned my face toward Him who originated the heavens and the earth, as a Hanif, and I am not one of the polytheists.' (6:78-79)."

"Abraham only intended by what he said to demonstrate to them the falsehood of their religion and to prove to them that worship (العبادة) is not due to that which possesses the attributes of Venus, the moon, and the sun; rather, worship is due only to their Creator and the Creator of the heavens and the earth. What he used as an argument against his people was from what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, inspired him with and gave him, as He, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **وَتِلْكَ حُجَّتُنا آتَيْناها إِبْراهِيمَ عَلى قَوْمِهِ** (This was Our argument, which We gave to Abraham against his people)." Al-Ma'mun said: "May Allah reward you greatly, O son of the Messenger of Allah."

**I say:** The support for this narration through its content for several matters we derived from the context of the noble verses is evident. Some other supporting narrations will follow. As for what is in the narration regarding Abraham's (عليه‌السلام) saying: **هذا رَبِّي** (This is my lord) being uttered by way of denial and inquiry rather than reporting and affirmation, it is one of the views previously mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of the verses, which he (عليه‌السلام) presented to refute al-Ma'mun's argument. This does not negate the validity of other views, should there be any, as will follow.

Similarly, his saying: "Because setting is an attribute of the originated (المحدث)," etc., is not explicit in stating that the argument is derived from the setting being an originated event (الحادث), as some have mentioned. It is possible that the argument is derived from the "lack of love" (عدم الحب), the basis of which is that setting is an attribute of the originated which should not be the object of love. So understand this.

In *Kamal al-Din*: My father and Ibn al-Walid together from Sa'd from Ibn Yazid from Ibn Abi Umayr from Hisham ibn Salim from Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), who said: "The father of Abraham was an astrologer (منجم) for Nimrod ibn Canaan, and Nimrod would not act except upon his advice. He looked at the stars one night and in the morning said: 'I saw something wondrous this night.' Nimrod asked: 'What is it?' He replied: 'I saw that a child will be born in this land of ours, at whose hands our destruction shall be, and it will not be long before he is conceived.' Nimrod was amazed and asked: 'Have any women conceived him yet?' He said: 'No.' Among the knowledge he was given was that the child would be burned with fire, but he was not given the knowledge that Allah would save him."

He said: "So he separated the women from the men, leaving no woman except that she was placed in the city so that men could not reach them. However, Abraham's father approached his wife and she conceived him. He thought he was the one [the child's father], so he sent for midwives (القوابل) saying: 'There is nothing in a womb but that we know of it.' They examined Abraham's mother, but Allah, Blessed and Exalted is His mention, caused what was in the womb to adhere to the back (الظهر), so they said: 'We see nothing in her belly.'"

"When Abraham's mother gave birth, his father wanted to take him to Nimrod. His wife said to him: 'Do not take your son to Nimrod for him to kill him. Let me take him to some caves (الغيران) and place him there until his time comes, so that you are not the one who kills your son.' He said to her: 'Go then.' She took him to a cave, nursed him, placed a rock at the cave's entrance, and then left him. Allah placed his provision in his thumb, so he would suck it and drink milk. He grew in a day as others grow in a week, in a week as others grow in a month, and in a month as others grow in a year. He remained there as long as Allah willed."

"Then his mother said to his father: 'If you would permit me to go to that boy and see him, I would do so.' He said: 'Do so.' She went to the cave and found Abraham, his eyes shining like two lamps. She took him, pressed him to her chest, nursed him, and then left him. His father asked her about the boy, and she said: 'I have buried him in the earth.'"

"She continued to make excuses to go out for needs and go to Abraham, pressing him to her and nursing him, then returning. When he began to move about, his mother came to him as she used to and did as she used to do. When she wanted to leave, he grabbed her garment. She said: 'What is the matter with you?' He said: 'Take me with you.' She said: 'Until I seek permission from your father.' Abraham remained in concealment (الغيبة), his person hidden and his affair kept secret, until he appeared and proclaimed the command of Allah, Blessed and Exalted is His mention, and Allah manifested His power through him."

**I say:** It is narrated in *Qisas al-Anbiya* from al-Saduq from his father and Ibn al-Walid, then he cited the chain to Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), who said: "Azar, the uncle of Abraham, was an astrologer for Nimrod, and he would not act except upon his advice. He said: 'I saw something wondrous this night.' He asked: 'What is it?' He said: 'A child will be born in this land of ours, at whose hands our destruction shall be.' So he separated the men from the women. Terah (تارخ) had approached Abraham's mother and she conceived..." then he cited the rest of the tradition.

The author of *al-Bihar* (Majlisi), considering the identical chain of the two traditions and the identical content except regarding Abraham's father, said: "It appears that what al-Rawandi narrated is this very report, but he altered it to align with the principles of the Imamiyyah (أصول الإمامية)." End quote. He then interpreted the narration and those of similar content—which indicate that the idolater Azar was Abraham's biological father—as being based on dissimulation (التقية).

Similar content has been narrated by al-Qummi in his *Tafsir* and al-Ayyashi in his *Tafsir*. It is also narrated through Sunni paths from Mujahid, and recorded by al-Tabari in his *Tarikh* and al-Tha'labi in *Qisas al-Anbiya* from the general body of the predecessors (السلف) and people of knowledge.

In any case, what ought to be said is that the scholars of Hadith and historical reports seem to be in consensus that Abraham (عليه‌السلام) was hidden in a subterranean chamber (سرب) in his early life for fear that King Nimrod would kill him. He then emerged after a time and argued with his father and his people regarding the idols, the star, the moon, and the sun, and argued with the king regarding his claim to divinity. It has already been mentioned that the context of the verses of the story supports this meaning.

As for Abraham's father, historians have mentioned that his name was Terah (تارخ)—with either a plain or dotted *ha*. Azar was either his title, the name of an idol, or a descriptive term of praise or blame in their language, meaning "the supporter" or "the lame," with which Abraham described him.

They mentioned that this polytheist whom the Quran calls Abraham's father and mentions his argument with him was Terah, his biological father and true parent. A number of scholars of Hadith and theology from the Sunnis agreed with them on this, while a group of them disagreed. The Shi'a are virtually in consensus—or are in consensus—on the contrary, except for what appears from some traditionalists who included those reports in their books. The primary argument of those who say that the polytheist Azar was not Abraham's father, but rather his uncle or maternal grandfather, is the reports narrated through the paths of both sects stating that the ancestors of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were all monotheists (موحدين) and none among them was a polytheist. The dispute between the two parties has been lengthy.

**I say:** Research of this style, however it concludes, lies outside the scope of exegetical research (البحث التفسيري), even if researchers from both sides find it necessary to present it and conclude the truth of what it yields. However, we are in no need of that, as it has already been established that the verses indicate that the polytheist Azar mentioned by Allah the Exalted in these verses of Surah al-An'am was not the true father of Abraham (عليه‌السلام).

Thus, the narrations indicating that Azar was his true father—despite the variations in their narration of the story—are reports that contradict the Book (مخالفة للكتاب) and are not to be given weight. There is no need, therefore, to interpret them as dissimulation (التقية), even if such an interpretation were valid given the differences among the people.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ** (Thus did We show Abraham...) the verse—he said: My father narrated to me from Isma'il ibn Marar from Yunus ibn Abd al-Rahman from Hisham from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), who said: "The earth and those upon it were unveiled (كشط) for him, as were the heaven and those within it, the angel that carries it, the Throne (العرش) and those upon it. He did the same for His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام)."

**I say:** Similar to this was narrated in *Basa'ir al-Darajat* through two paths from Abdullah ibn Muskan and Abu Basir from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), and through one path from Abd al-Rahim from Imam al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام). Al-Ayyashi narrated it from Zurarah and Abu Basir from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), and from Zurarah and Abd al-Rahim al-Qasir from Imam al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام). It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, and al-Suddi among the early commentators. In the discussion on the Throne (العرش), the tradition of Ali (عليه‌السلام) narrated in *al-Kafi* regarding the meaning of the Throne will follow, in which he said: "Those who carry the Throne and those around it are the scholars whom Allah has made to carry His knowledge." He said: "It is the kingdom (الملكوت) which Allah showed to His chosen ones and showed to His Friend (عليه‌السلام), saying: **وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ** (Thus did We show Abraham the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certainty)."

In this tradition is an explanation of the other reports narrated regarding the showing of the kingdom (إراءة الملكوت) and a support for what we presented in the previous commentary. A full explanation of this tradition will come to you in Surah al-A'raf, Allah the Exalted willing.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*: From Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), who said: "When he saw the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, he looked and saw a man committing adultery, so he prayed against him and he died. Then he saw another and prayed against him and he died, until he had seen three and prayed against them and they died. Then Allah revealed to him: 'O Abraham, your prayer is answered, so do not pray against My servants. For if I had willed, I would not have created them. I have created My creation in three categories: a servant who worships Me and associates nothing with Me; a servant who worships other than Me, but he shall not escape Me; and a servant who worships other than Me, but I shall bring forth from his loins one who worships Me.'"

**I say:** The narration is widely transmitted (مستفيضة). It was narrated in *al-Kafi* with a chain from Abu Basir from him (عليه‌السلام), and by al-Saduq in *al-Ilal* from him (عليه‌السلام), and by al-Tabarsi in *al-Ihtijaj* from Imam al-Askari (عليه‌السلام). It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Mardawayh from Ali from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and from Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Shu'ab* through the path of Shahr ibn Hawshab from Mu'adh ibn Jabal from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and from several commentators as a suspended (موقوف) report.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*: From Muhammad ibn Muslim from one of the two [Imams al-Baqir or al-Sadiq] (عليهما‌السلام), who said regarding Abraham when he saw a star: "He was only seeking his Lord and had not reached disbelief (كفرا). Anyone among the people who contemplates in such a manner is in his position."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: He said: Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) was asked about Abraham's saying: **هذا رَبِّي** (This is my lord)—"Did he commit polytheism (أشرك) in his saying: **هذا رَبِّي**?" He replied: "Whoever says this today is a polytheist (مشرك), but there was no polytheism from Abraham; he was only in search of his Lord—whereas from anyone else, it is polytheism."

**I say:** The one who is a "seeker" (طالب) is contrasted with the one for whom the explanation is complete and the clear argument has been established; such a person is not a seeker. It is not for the non-seeker to hypothesize that which contains polytheism.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*: From Hujr, who said: Al-Ala ibn Siyabah sent to ask Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) about Abraham's saying: **هذا رَبِّي**, and that whoever says this today is a polytheist in our view. He said: "There was no polytheism from Abraham; he was only in search of his Lord. From anyone else, it is polytheism."

In the same source: From Muhammad ibn Humran, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah regarding what He reported of Abraham: **هذا رَبِّي**. He said: "He did not mean anything by it; he intended other than what he said."

**I say:** What is apparently meant is that he intended that his saying **هذا رَبِّي** should not exceed its own conceptualization, and that there was no meaning behind it that it reported—meaning he said it by way of hypothesis (الافتراض) or conceding the claim (تسليم المدعى) to demonstrate its falsehood through the falsehood of its implications, as previously indicated.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ** — "Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing..." (6:82). Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Daraqutni in *al-Afrad*, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, who said: "When this verse was revealed: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ**, it was difficult for the people, and they said: 'O Messenger of Allah, which of us does not wrong himself?'"

He said: "It is not as you intend. Have you not heard what the righteous servant [Luqman] said: **إِنَّ الشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ** — 'Polytheism is indeed a great wrongdoing'? (31:13). It is only polytheism (الشرك)."

**I say:** The "righteous servant" refers to Luqman, according to what Allah the Exalted recounted of his saying in Surah Luqman. In the tradition is an indication that Surah al-An'am was revealed after Surah Luqman. It has already been mentioned that the intended meaning being polytheism is only a matter of application (الانطباق) according to the instance, for polytheism is a sin to which no forgiveness attaches at all, unlike others, whatever they may be. The evidence for what we have mentioned is found in the following narrations.

In the same source: Ahmad, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu'ab al-Iman* narrated from Jarir ibn Abdillah, who said: "We went out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When we were outside Medina, a rider came galloping toward us. He reached us and gave greetings. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) asked him: 'Where have you come from?' He said: 'From my family, my children, and my kinsfolk; I seek the Messenger of Allah.' He said: 'You have found him.' He said: 'Teach me, what is faith (الإيمان)?' He said: 'That you bear witness that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah, that you perform the prayer, give the zakat, fast Ramadan, and perform the Hajj to the House.' He said: 'I have affirmed this.'"

"Then his camel's leg entered a hole of a desert rat and it fell, and the man fell on his head and died. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'This is one of those who did little and were rewarded much. This is one of those of whom Allah said: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ** — "Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be safety, and they are the [rightly] guided." (6:82). I saw the wide-eyed houris (الحور العين) placing the fruits of Paradise into his mouth, so I knew the man died hungry.'"

**I say:** It was also narrated from al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi and Ibn Abi Hatim from Ibn Abbas in a similar vein. Al-Ayyashi narrated it in his *Tafsir* from Jabir al-Ju'fi from someone who narrated it from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) similarly.

In the same source: Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Ibrahim al-Taymi: "A man asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about it, and he remained silent until a man came and embraced Islam. He did not remain long before he fought and was martyred. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'This man is among them—among those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing.'"

In the same source: Al-Faryabi, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ**. He said: "This verse was revealed concerning Abraham and his companions specifically—it is not for this nation (الأمة)."

**I say:** The narration, in its apparent sense, does not align with the universal principles (الأصول الكلية) derived from the Book and the Sunnah. For the verse, in its content, does not contain a specific ruling (حكم خاص) restricted to one nation over another, like the secondary legislative rulings (الأحكام الفرعية التشريعية) that might be specific to one time over another. As for faith (الإيمان), with its effects across its levels, and likewise wrongdoing (الظلم) across its levels, with their negative effects on faith—these are matters embedded in the human primordial nature (الفطرة الإنسانية) that do not vary with the change of times and nations.

Some commentators said in justifying the tradition: "Perhaps his meaning is that Allah specifically granted Abraham and his people safety for their monotheists from the punishment of the Hereafter absolutely, not just safety from eternal residence therein. Perhaps the reason for this—if true—is that Allah the Exalted did not charge the people of Abraham with anything other than monotheism (التوحيد), sufficing with the training of their rigorous civil laws (شرائعهم المدنية) for them in personal, moral, and other affairs."

"Researchers have discovered the laws of Hammurabi, the righteous king who lived in the time of Abraham and whom Abraham blessed and from whom he took tithes—as in the Book of Genesis. They found them to be like the Torah in most of their rulings. As for Allah's imposing the Hajj through the tongue of Abraham, that was for the people of his son Ishmael, not for his Chaldean people. As for this nation, among its monotheists are those who are punished for sins according to their measure, because they were addressed with a complete law (شريعة كاملة) for the establishment of which they are held accountable." End quote.

In his words is an arbitrariness (تحكم) that is not hidden. It has already been mentioned that this King Hammurabi lived around the year 1700 BC, while Abraham lived around 2000 BC approximately, as he mentioned.

Hammurabi, though he was a righteous king in his religion, just toward his subjects, and committed to the implementation of laws he established and executed excellently in his kingdom—which are said to be the oldest established civil laws—was nevertheless a polytheist. He sought aid from the gods of the polytheists in what he wrote after completing his law, according to what was found in the ruins of Babylon. The gods he mentioned in his statement placed at the end of the law—whom he thanked for giving him the great kingdom and enabling him to spread justice and establish the law, and whom he sought help from to preserve his law from vanishing or alteration—were "Marduk," the god of gods, "Ea," the god of law and justice, the god "Zamama," the goddess "Ishtar," the goddess of war, "Shamash," the judge-god in heaven and earth, "Sin," the god of the heavens, "Hadad," the god of fertility, "Nergal," the god of victory, "Bel," the god of destiny, the goddess "Beltis," the goddess "Ninu," the god "Sagila," and others.

What he mentioned—that Allah did not charge the people of Abraham with anything other than monotheism, sufficing with the training of their civil laws, etc.—is belied by the fact that the Quran recounts from the tongue of Abraham (عليه‌السلام) the prayer (الصلاة), as in his supplications in Surah Ibrahim. It mentions that Allah revealed to him the doing of good deeds (فعل الخيرات) and the giving of zakat, as in Surah al-Anbiya; that He legislated the Hajj and permitted the meat of livestock, as in Surah al-Hajj; that among his laws was the withdrawal from the polytheists, as in Surah al-Mumtahanah; and that he forbade every wrongdoing (ظلم) that the primordial nature does not accept, as in Surah al-An'am and others. Among his laws was purification (التطهر), as indicated by Surah al-Hajj. Reports have stated that he (عليه‌السلام) legislated the *Hanifiyyah*, which consists of ten characteristics: five in the head and five in the body, among which is circumcision. He used to greet with peace (السلام), as in Surah Hud and Maryam.

Allah the Exalted has said: **مِلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْراهِيمَ** — "the faith of your father, Abraham" (22:78), and said: **قُلْ بَلْ مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً** — "Say, 'Rather [we follow] the creed of Abraham, a Hanif'" (2:135). Thus, He described this religion, with its roots and branches, as the creed (ملة) of Abraham (عليه‌السلام). While this does not indicate that this religion, with its detailed rulings, was legislated in the time of Abraham (عليه‌السلام)—rather the opposite is true, as indicated by His saying: **شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى** — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus" (42:13)—it does indicate that his laws return to a root or universal roots (أصول كلية) to which the primordial nature guides, which it accepts and commands, or does not accept and forbids. Allah said at the end of this Surah, after mentioning arguments against polytheism and a set of universal commands and prohibitions, addressing His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): **قُلْ إِنَّنِي هَدانِي رَبِّي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ ، دِيناً قِيَماً مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً وَما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — "Say, 'Indeed my Lord has guided me to a straight path, the upright religion, the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists.'" (6:161).

If the matter were as he mentioned—that Allah did not legislate a law for Abraham (عليه‌السلام) but sufficed with the existing civil law, which was the law of Hammurabi—then the mentioned law would have been endorsed and approved by Allah, and would have been part of the religion of Abraham (عليه‌السلام), and indeed the Islamic religion legislated in the Quran, for it is the creed of Abraham, a Hanif. It would thus have been one of the divine laws and a type of heavenly book.

The truth, in which there is no doubt, is that divine revelation (الوحي الإلهي) taught the past prophets and their nations universal principles (أصول كلية) regarding the worldly life and the Hereafter, such as types of worship (العبادة) and universal traditions (سنن كلية) regarding good and evil. A person of sound intellect (سليم العقل) is guided to identify these through righteous social interaction, avoidance of wrongdoing and extravagance, and not aiding the arrogant, and the like. They were then commanded to enter societies with this equipment they were provided with, calling to the adoption of good and righteousness and the rejection of evil, indecency, and corruption—whether the societies they entered were governed by the despotism of tyrants or the mercy and organized policy of just rulers.

Detailed rulings (تفاصيل الأحكام) were not legislated before the appearance of the Islamic religion except in the Torah. In it are rulings, some of which resemble parts of Hammurabi's law. However, the Torah was revealed by Allah to Moses (عليه‌السلام) and was preserved among the Children of Israel. They lost it during the tribulation of Nebuchadnezzar, which annihilated their gathering and destroyed their Temple. None remained of them except a small group led into captivity to Babylon, where they were enslaved and dwelt until King Cyrus conquered Babylon and released them from captivity. He permitted them to return to Jerusalem and allowed Ezra the Priest to write the Torah for them after its copies had been destroyed and the texts of its insights forgotten, while they had become accustomed to the laws of Babylon current among the Chaldeans.

Given this situation, how can it be judged that Allah endorsed many of Hammurabi's laws in the Mosaic law, when the Quran only confirms some of what is in this Torah? Beyond all that, there is no barrier to some non-heavenly laws containing some righteous elements and true rulings.

In *al-Kafi*: With its chain to Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ**. He said: "It is polytheism (الشرك)."

In the same source: Through another path from Abu Basir from him (عليه‌السلام) regarding the verse, he said: "With doubt (بشك)."

**I say:** Al-Ayyashi also narrated it in his *Tafsir* from Abu Basir from him (عليه‌السلام).

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*: From Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), who said: "I asked about the saying of Allah: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ**. He said: 'We seek refuge in Allah, O Abu Basir, from being among those who taint their faith with wrongdoing.' Then he said: 'Those are the Kharijites (الخوارج) and their companions.'"

In the same source: From Ya'qub ibn Shu'ayb from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying: **وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ**. He said: "Misguidance (الضلال) and what is above it."

**I say:** It is as if what is meant by "misguidance" in the narration is polytheism, which is the root of all wrongdoing above it and the sins and injustices that exceed it. Or what is meant by misguidance is the minimum by which wrongdoing is realized through sins, and by "what is above it" is polytheism, which is the severe level of misguidance, for every sin is misguidance.

The narrations—as you see—vary in the interpretation of "wrongdoing" (الظلم) in the verse. Sometimes they interpret it as polytheism, sometimes as doubt, and sometimes as what the Kharijites follow. In some, it is said that the guardianship (ولاية) of their enemies is part of it. All of this serves as evidence for what we previously established: that wrongdoing in the verse is absolute (مطلق), and in its absoluteness, it possesses levels according to the degrees of understanding.

## (A Discourse on the Story of Abraham (عليه‌السلام) and His Personality)

This includes various researches: some Quranic, others scientific, historical, and otherwise.

**1 - The Story of Abraham (عليه‌السلام) in the Quran:** Abraham (عليه‌السلام), from his childhood until the beginning of his discernment, lived in isolation from the society of his people. He then went out to them and joined his father, finding him and his people worshipping idols. He did not accept that from him or them. His primordial nature (الفطرة) was pure and untainted, supported by Allah the Glorified with true witnessing (الشهود الحق) and the showing of the kingdom (ملكوت) of everything—in short, with true speech and righteous action.

He began to argue with his father regarding his worship of idols, calling him to reject them, to unify Allah the Glorified, and to follow him so that he might guide him to the straight path (مستقيم الصراط) and distance him from the guardianship of Satan (ولاية الشيطان). He continued to argue with him and persist until his father rebuked him and drove him away, threatening to stone him if he did not cease mentioning his gods with ill and turning away from them.

Abraham (عليه‌السلام) acted with kindness toward him, out of compassion and affection—for he possessed a noble character and pleasing speech—so he bid him peace and promised to seek forgiveness for him and to withdraw from him and his people and what they worshipped besides Allah. (19:41-48).

On another front, he was arguing with the people regarding the matter of the idols (21:51-56, 26:69-77, 37:83-87). He also argued with other groups among them who worshipped the sun, the moon, and the star regarding them, until he established the truth against them. The news of his deviation from the idols and gods spread (6:74-82). One day, the people went out to a collective worship outside the city. He feigned illness and did not go out with them, staying behind. He entered the house of idols and turned upon their gods, striking them with his right hand, reducing them to fragments except for a large one of them, that they might perhaps return to it. When they returned and saw what had happened to their gods and searched for who had committed it, they said: "We heard a youth mentioning them, who is called Abraham."

They brought him to their assembly, bringing him before the eyes of the people that they might bear witness. They interrogated him, saying: "Was it you who did this to our gods, O Abraham?" He said: "Rather, this big one of them did it; so ask them, if they can speak." He had left the largest of the idols and had not broken it, placing the axe on its shoulder or near it, so that the situation would testify that it was the one that broke the other idols.

He (عليه‌السلام) only said that while knowing they would not believe him, and they knew it was an inanimate object (جماد) incapable of that. However, he said what he said so that he might follow it with his saying: "So ask them, if they can speak," until they would confess with explicit speech that they were inanimate objects without life or consciousness. Therefore, when they heard his words, they returned to themselves and said: "Indeed, you are the wrongdoers." Then they were confounded: "You certainly know that these do not speak." He said: "Do you then worship besides Allah that which neither harms you nor benefits you? Fie on you and on what you worship besides Allah! Do you not exercise your intellect? Do you worship what you yourselves carve, while Allah created you and what you make?"

They said: "Burn him and help your gods!" So they built a structure for him and kindled a blazing fire within it. The people participated collectively in his affair and threw him into the blaze, but Allah made it coolness and peace for him and frustrated their plot (21:57-70, 37:88-98). During these events, he was brought before the king, whom the people worshipped and took as a lord. He argued with Abraham regarding his Lord. Abraham said: "My Lord is He who gives life and causes death." The king used a sophistry (غالطه) and said: "I give life and cause death," such as by killing a prisoner or releasing him. Abraham then argued with him with the most explicit point that would cut off his sophistry, saying: "Indeed Allah brings the sun from the east, so bring it from the west." Thus was the disbeliever confounded (2:258).

Then, when Allah saved him from the fire, he began to call to the Hanif religion, the religion of monotheism (التوحيد). A small group believed in him, among whom Allah named Lot, and among them was his wife with whom he migrated. He had married her before leaving the land for the Holy Land.

Then he (عليه‌السلام) and the believers with him disassociated themselves from their people. He disassociated himself from Azar, whom he used to call father but who was not his true parent. He migrated with his wife and Lot to the Holy Land to call to Allah the Glorified without any opponent from his coarse and unjust people (60:4, 21:71). There, Allah the Glorified gave him the glad tidings of Ishmael, and of Isaac, and after Isaac, Jacob. He had grown old and reached an advanced age. Ishmael was born to him, then Isaac was born to him, and Allah the Glorified blessed him, his two sons, and their children.

Then, by a command from his Lord, he went to the land of Mecca, which was a valley without cultivation. He settled his son Ishmael there while he was a child and returned to the Holy Land. Ishmael grew up there, and a group of Arabs residing there gathered around him, and thus the city of Mecca was built.

He (عليه‌السلام) would occasionally visit Ishmael in the land of Mecca before the building of Mecca and the House, and after that (2:126, 14:35-41). Then he built the Kaaba, the Sacred House, with the participation of Ishmael. It was the first house established for the people by Allah, blessed and a guidance for all the worlds; in it are clear signs, the station of Abraham, and whoever enters it is safe (2:127-129, 3:96-97). He announced the Hajj among the people and legislated the rites of Hajj (22:26-30).

Then Allah commanded him to sacrifice his son Ishmael (عليه‌السلام). He went out with him for the rite. When the boy reached the age of running with him, he said: "O my son! I see in a dream that I am sacrificing you." He said: "O my father! Do what you are commanded. You will find me, if Allah wills, to be patient." When they had both submitted and he had laid him down on his forehead, he was called: "O Abraham! You have indeed fulfilled the vision." And Allah the Glorified ransomed him with a great sacrifice (37:101-107).

The last of what the noble Quran recounts of his stories (عليه‌السلام) are his supplications during some of the days of his presence in Mecca, recorded in Surah Ibrahim (14:35-41). The last mentioned therein is his saying (عليه‌السلام): **رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوالِدَيَّ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْحِسابُ** — "Our Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and the faithful, on the day when the reckoning is held." (14:41).

**Abraham's Status with Allah the Glorified and His Station of Servitude:** Allah the Exalted praised Abraham (عليه‌السلام) in His speech with the most beautiful praise and lauded his trial for His sake with the most eloquent commendation. He repeated his mention by name in over sixty places in His Book and mentioned many of His gifts and blessings upon him. Here are some of them: Allah gave him his rectitude (رشده) from before (21:51). He chose him in this world, and in the Hereafter he is among the righteous. When his Lord said to him, "Submit (أسلم)," he said, "I submit to the Lord of all the worlds" (2:130-131). He is the one who turned his face toward his Lord as a Hanif and was not of the polytheists (6:79). He is the one whose heart found tranquility (اطمأن قلبه) in Allah and who attained certainty (أيقن) in Him through what Allah showed him of the kingdom of the heavens and the earth (2:260, 6:75).

Allah took him as a friend (خليلا) (4:125) and placed His mercy and blessings upon him and the people of his house. He described him as one who fulfilled (التوفية) (53:37). He praised him as being clement (حليم), compassionate (أواه), and penitent (منيب) (10:73-75). He praised him as being a nation (أمة), obedient (قانتا) to Allah, a Hanif, and not of the polytheists, grateful for His blessings; He chose him and guided him to a straight path, gave him good in this world, and in the Hereafter he is among the righteous (16:120-122).

He was a truthful man (صديقا) and a prophet (19:41). Allah counted him among His faithful servants and among the virtuous (المحسنين) and bid him peace (37:83-111). He is among those whom He described as possessing strength (الأيدي) and insight (الأبصار) and whom He distinguished with a pure quality: the remembrance of the Abode (38:45-46).

Allah made him an Imam (إماما) for the people (2:124). He made him one of the five "Possessors of Resolve" (أولي العزم) who were given the Book and the Law (33:7, 42:13, 87:18-19). Allah gave him knowledge (العلم), wisdom (الحكمة), the Book, sovereignty (الملك), and guidance, and made it a lasting word among his posterity (4:54, 6:74-90, 43:28). He placed prophethood and the Book in his progeny (57:26) and granted him a mention of truth (لسان صدق) among the later generations (26:84, 19:50). These are the summaries of the divine offices and stations of servitude (مقامات العبودية) that Allah the Glorified granted him. The noble Quran did not detail the attributes and miracles of any of the honored prophets and messengers as it detailed his attributes and miracles (عليه‌السلام).

One should refer to the commentary of each of his mentioned stations in the specific places we have explained previously or will explain, Allah the Exalted willing; for engaging in that here would take us away from the purpose for which these researches were established.

Allah the Glorified has preserved his noble life and religious personality by naming this upright religion "Islam," just as he (عليه‌السلام) named it and attributed it to him. The Exalted said: **مِلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْراهِيمَ هُوَ سَمَّاكُمُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ مِنْ قَبْلُ** — "the faith of your father, Abraham. He named you 'Muslims' beforehand" (22:78). And He said: **قُلْ إِنَّنِي هَدانِي رَبِّي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ دِيناً قِيَماً مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً وَما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — "Say, 'Indeed my Lord has guided me to a straight path, the upright religion, the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists.'" (6:161).

He made the Kaaba, the Sacred House which he built, the direction of prayer (قبلة) for all the worlds, and legislated the rites of Hajj, which are in reality acts representing the story of his settling his son and the mother of his child, the sacrifice of his son Ishmael, his striving toward his Lord, his turning to Him, and his bearing of harm and trial for His sake, as previously indicated in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذْ جَعَلْنَا الْبَيْتَ مَثابَةً لِلنَّاسِ** (And when We made the House a place of return for the people) the verse (2:125) in the first volume of the book.

**3 - His Blessed Impact on Human Society:** Among his abundant favors is that the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) traces back to him wherever it is and with whomever it is. For the religion described as monotheism today is the religion of the Jews, which traces back to the Speaker (الكليم) Moses ibn Imran (عليه‌السلام), whose lineage traces back to Israel, Jacob ibn Isaac ibn Abraham (عليه‌السلام). And the religion of Christianity traces back to the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary (عليه‌السلام), who is from the progeny of Abraham (عليه‌السلام). And the religion of Islam, whose proclaimer is Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), whose lineage traces back to Ishmael the Sacrificed, son of Abraham the Friend (عليه‌السلام). Thus, the religion of monotheism in the world is his good and blessed impact. Observed in Islam from his laws are the prayer, zakat, Hajj, the permissibility of the meat of livestock, disassociation from the enemies of Allah, the greeting of peace, and the ten purifications of the pure *Hanifiyyah*: five in the head and five in the body. As for those in the head: trimming the mustache, letting the beard grow, cutting the hair, using the tooth-stick (السواك), and using the toothpick (الخلال). As for those in the body: shaving body hair, circumcision, clipping the nails, the major ritual bath (الغسل) from ritual impurity, and purification with water.

Exhaustive research supports that the righteous traditions (السنن الصالحة) of belief and action in human society, whatever they may be, are among the good impacts of prophethood, as repeatedly indicated in previous discussions. Thus, Abraham (عليه‌السلام) has beautiful favors upon all of humanity today, whether they know it or not.

**4 - What the Current Torah Recounts Concerning Abraham:** The Torah says: Terah (Abraham's father) lived seventy years and begot Abram, Nahor, and Haran. These are the generations of Terah: Terah begot Abram, Nahor, and Haran. Haran begot Lot. Haran died before his father in the land of his birth, in "Ur" of the Chaldeans. Abram and Nahor took wives for themselves—the name of Abram's wife was "Sarai," and the name of Nahor's wife was Milkah, daughter of Haran, the father of Milkah and Iscah. Sarai was barren and had no child. Terah took Abram his son, and Lot son of Haran, his grandson, and Sarai his daughter-in-law, the wife of Abram his son. They went out together from Ur of the Chaldeans to go to the land of Canaan. They came to Haran and stayed there. The days of Terah were two hundred and five years, and Terah died in Haran.

The Torah says: The Lord said to Abram: "Go from your land, from your kindred, and from your father's house to the land that I will show you. I will make you a great nation, I will bless you and make your name great, and you shall be a blessing. I will bless those who bless you, and him who curses you I will curse, and in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed." So Abram went as the Lord had told him, and Lot went with him. Abram was seventy-five years old when he departed from Haran. Abram took Sarai his wife and Lot his brother's son, and all their possessions that they had gathered, and the souls they had acquired in Haran, and they set out to go to the land of Canaan. They came to the land of Canaan.

Abram passed through the land to the place of "Shechem," to the "Oak of Moreh." The Canaanites were then in the land. The Lord appeared to Abram and said: "To your offspring I will give this land." So he built there an altar to the Lord who had appeared to him. Then he moved from there to the mountain east of "Bethel" and pitched his tent, with "Bethel" on the west and "Ai" on the east. He built there an altar to the Lord and called upon the name of the Lord. Then Abram journeyed on, still going toward the South.

There was a famine in the land, so Abram went down to Egypt to sojourn there, for the famine was severe in the land. When he was about to enter Egypt, he said to Sarai his wife: "I know that you are a woman beautiful in appearance. When the Egyptians see you, they will say, 'This is his wife,' and they will kill me but let you live. Say you are my sister, that it may go well with me because of you, and that my soul may live for your sake." When Abram entered Egypt, the Egyptians saw that the woman was very beautiful. Pharaoh's officials saw her and praised her to Pharaoh, and the woman was taken into Pharaoh's house. He treated Abram well for her sake, and he acquired sheep, oxen, male donkeys, male and female servants, female donkeys, and camels.

The Lord struck Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai, Abram's wife. Pharaoh called Abram and said: "What is this you have done to me? Why did you not tell me she was your wife? Why did you say, 'She is my sister,' so that I took her to be my wife? Now then, here is your wife; take her and go." He gave men orders concerning him, and they sent him on his way with his wife and all that he had.

Then the Torah mentioned: that Abram went up from Egypt with Sarai and Lot, and with them were sheep, servants, and great wealth. They came to "Bethel," the place where his tent had been pitched between "Bethel" and "Ai." After a time, he and Lot separated because the land could not support them both. Abram dwelt in Canaan—the Canaanites and Perizzites were dwelling there—and Lot settled in the land of Sodom.

Then it mentioned: that in those days, war broke out in the land of Sodom between "Amraphel" king of Shinar with three other kings, and Bera king of Sodom with four allied kings. The king of Sodom and those with him were soundly defeated and fled from the land after many were killed. Their wealth was plundered and their women and children were taken captive. Among those captured was Lot and all his family, and his wealth was plundered.

The Torah says: One who had escaped came and told Abram the Hebrew, who was living by the "Oaks of Mamre" the Amorite, brother of "Eshcol" and "Aner," who were allies of Abram. When Abram heard that his kinsman had been taken captive, he led forth his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen, and went in pursuit as far as "Dan." He divided his forces against them by night, he and his servants, and defeated them and pursued them to "Hobah," north of Damascus. He brought back all the goods, and also brought back his kinsman Lot with his goods, and the women and the people.

The king of Sodom went out to meet him after his return from the defeat of "Chedorlaomer" and the kings who were with him, at the Valley of Shaveh (that is, the King's Valley). And Melchizedek king of "Salem" brought out bread and wine; he was priest of God Most High. And he blessed him and said: "Blessed be Abram by God Most High, Possessor of heaven and earth; and blessed be God Most High, who has delivered your enemies into your hand!" And Abram gave him a tenth of everything.

The King of Sodom said to Abram, "Give me the persons, and take the property for yourself." But Abram said to the King of Sodom, "I have raised my hand to the Lord, God Most High, Possessor of heaven and earth, that I will take nothing, from a thread to a sandal strap, nor anything that is yours, lest you should say, 'I have made Abram rich.' I will take nothing but what the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men who went with me: Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre; let them take their portion."

The account continues until it says: Now Sarai had borne him no children. She had an Egyptian maidservant whose name was Hagar. So Sarai said to Abram, "See now, the Lord has restrained me from bearing children. Please, go in to my maidservant; perhaps I shall obtain children by her." And Abram heeded the voice of Sarai. Then Sarai, Abram’s wife, took Hagar her Egyptian maidservant, after Abram had dwelt ten years in the land of Canaan, and gave her to her husband Abram to be his wife. So he went in to Hagar, and she conceived.

Then it mentions: When Hagar saw that she had conceived, she despised Sarai and acted arrogantly toward her. Sarai complained about this to Abram,

so Abram delegated her matter to her, and Hagar fled from her. An angel met her and commanded her to return to her mistress, informing her that she would bear a male child and should call his name Ishmael, because the Lord had heard of her affliction. The Exalted said, after mentioning the story of breaking the idols and his being cast into the fire and it being made cool and safe: **"They desired to outwit him, but We made them the lowermost. He said, ‘I am going to my Lord; He will guide me. My Lord! Give me [a son], one of the righteous.’ So We gave him the good news of a forbearing lad. When he was old enough to assist him, he said, ‘My son! I see in a dream that I am sacrificing you. See what you think.’ He said, ‘O my father! Do whatever you are commanded to do. If Allah wishes, you will find me to be patient.’ So when they had both surrendered [to Allah’s will] and he had laid him down on his forehead, We called out to him, ‘O Abraham! You have indeed fulfilled the vision! Thus do We reward the virtuous! This was indeed a manifest trial.’ Then We ransomed him with a great sacrifice, and We left for him a good name among posterity. ‘Peace be to Abraham!’ Thus do We reward the virtuous. Indeed he was one of Our faithful servants. And We gave him the good news of Isaac, a prophet, one of the righteous. And We blessed him and Isaac, and among the descendants of the two there are the virtuous and those who are manifest wrongdoers to themselves."** [Qarai: 37:98-113].

One who contemplates these noble verses finds no choice but to admit that the one to be sacrificed is the one whom Allah, the Glorified, mentioned the glad tidings of in His saying: **"So We gave him the good news of a forbearing lad,"** and that the other glad tidings mentioned at the end in His saying: **"And We gave him the good news of Isaac, a prophet, one of the righteous,"** is different from the first glad tidings. The one announced in the second—who is Isaac (peace be upon him)—is other than the one announced in the first, which was followed by the mention of the story of his sacrifice.

As for the narrations, those reported through Shiah chains from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) mention that the one to be sacrificed was Ishmael (peace be upon him). Those reported through Sunni chains are varied: one group mentions Ishmael and another mentions Isaac (peace be upon him), but you have seen that the first group is the one that accords with the Book.

Al-Tabari said in his *Tarikh*: "The predecessors among the scholars of the community of our Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) differed regarding which of his two sons Abraham was commanded to sacrifice. Some said: it was Isaac son of Abraham, and others said: it was Ishmael son of Abraham. Both views have been narrated from the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny); had one of them been authentic, we would not have bypassed it for another. However, the evidence from the Quran for the correctness of the narration reported from him (peace be upon him and his progeny) that he said: 'It is Isaac,' is clearer and more manifest than for the correctness of the other."

Until he said: "As for the indication from the Quran which we said points to Isaac being more correct, it is the saying of the Exalted reporting the prayer of His Friend Abraham when he left his people, migrating to his Lord toward Syria with his wife Sarah: **'I am going to my Lord; He will guide me. My Lord! Give me [a son], one of the righteous.'** This was before he knew Hagar and before she became the mother of Ishmael. Then our Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, followed that with the report of answering his prayer and giving him the good news of a forbearing lad, then the vision of Abraham that he was sacrificing that lad when he reached the age of assisting him.

We do not know in the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, any glad tidings to Abraham of a male child except for Isaac, and that is His saying: **'And his wife was standing by and she laughed; so We gave her the good news of Isaac, and of Jacob after Isaac'** and His saying: **'Then he felt a fear of them. They said, "Do not be afraid," and they gave him the good news of a learned boy. Then his wife came forward with a cry and slapped her face and said, "A barren old woman!"'**

Furthermore, this is the case in every place where the glad tidings to Abraham of a lad are mentioned; it only mentions Allah's glad tidings to him through his wife Sarah. Therefore, it is necessary that in His saying: **'So We gave him the good news of a forbearing lad,'** it should be similar to what is in the other surahs of the Quran regarding the glad tidings to him through his wife Sarah.

As for the reasoning of those who argued that Allah did not command the sacrifice of Isaac because the glad tidings from Allah had come before his birth regarding his birth and the birth of Jacob from him after him, it is a reasoning that does not necessitate the correctness of what they said. This is because Allah only commanded Abraham to sacrifice Isaac after Isaac reached the age of assisting, and it is possible that Jacob was born to him before his father was commanded to sacrifice him.

Likewise, there is no weight to the reasoning of those who argued from the horns of the ram that he saw hanging in the Kaaba, for it is not impossible that it was carried from Syria to the Kaaba and hung there." End of his words.

I wonder how it was hidden from him that when Abraham (peace be upon him) asked his Lord for a child upon his migration to Syria—while Sarah was with him and there was no news of Hagar at that time—he asked for it by saying: **"My Lord! Give me [a son], one of the righteous."** He asked his Lord for a child, and did not ask that He grant it to him from Sarah, such that the glad tidings mentioned after it must be interpreted as the glad tidings of Isaac. He only said: **"My Lord! Give me,"** and did not say: "My Lord! Give me from Sarah."

As for what he mentioned—that what is known from other places in the Book of Allah is the glad tidings of Isaac, so we must interpret the glad tidings in this place as him as well—besides what will come of the discussion regarding the other instances he pointed to, it is in itself an analogy (قياس) for which there is no evidence; rather, the evidence is to the contrary. For Allah, the Glorified, in these verses, when He mentioned the glad tidings of a forbearing lad and then mentioned the story of the sacrifice, He initiated a second mention of the glad tidings of Isaac. One who contemplates this context will not doubt that the one announced the second time is other than the one announced the first time. Abraham (peace be upon him) was given the glad tidings of another son before Isaac, and that is none other than Ishmael. All narrators, transmitters, and historians agree that Ishmael was born to Abraham before Isaac (peace be upon them both).

Among these [distortions] is the manifest contradiction in what the Torah mentions regarding the matter of Ishmael. It explicitly states that Ishmael was born to Abraham (peace be upon him) nearly fourteen years before Isaac was born to him, and that Abraham (peace be upon him) expelled him and his mother Hagar after the birth of Isaac when he mocked Sarah. Then it narrates the story of their settling in the valley, the depletion of the water Hagar carried, Ishmael's thirst, and then the angel showing her the water. One who looks and contemplates the story will not doubt that Ishmael was at that time a nursing infant—so you must refer to it and reflect upon it. This is what accords with what is reported in our narrations.

6 — The Noble Quran pays the utmost attention to the story of Abraham (peace be upon him) regarding himself and regarding his two noble sons, Ishmael and Isaac, and their descendants together. This is unlike what is encountered in the Torah, for it limits the reports about him to what relates to Isaac and the people of Israel, and does not pay attention to Ishmael except with some things that belittle his matter and disparage his status. Despite this, the little it reports is not free from contradiction: at one time it mentions Allah's address to Abraham (peace be upon him) that "your remaining seed is from Isaac," and at another time His address that "Allah has blessed your seed from the offspring of Ishmael and will make him a great nation." At one time it describes him as a wild man, his hand against people and people's hands against him, having grown up as an archer expelled from his father's house, and at another time it mentions that Allah is with him.

By reflecting on what has preceded of his story (peace be upon him) in the Quran, the answer to two objections raised against the Mighty Book becomes clear.

**The First Objection:** What was mentioned by some Orientalists (1) that the Quran in its Meccan surahs does not
