address the matter of Abraham and Ishmael (peace be upon them) except as it addresses the matter of other messengers—that they were upon the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), warning the people and calling them to Allah, the Glorified—without mentioning his building of the Kaaba, his connection to Ishmael, and their being callers of the Arabs to the religion of primordial nature (دين الفطرة) and the Hanifi creed (الملة الحنيفية). However, the Medinan surahs, such as *al-Baqarah*, *al-Hajj*, and others, mention Abraham and Ishmael connected by the bond of fatherhood and sonship, as the two fathers of the Arabs who legislated for them the religion of Islam and built the Kaaba, the Sacred House.

The secret of this difference—according to them—is that Muhammad had relied on the Jews in Mecca, but they soon adopted a hostile stance toward him, so he had no choice but to seek another supporter. There, a guided intelligence led him to a new role for the father of the Arabs, Abraham. By this, he was able to break free from the Judaism of his time to connect his rope to the Judaism of Abraham, counting him as the father of the Arabs, the builder of their religion, Islam, and the builder of their holy house in Mecca, since this city occupied most of his thinking. End of the summary.

The objector has disparaged himself with this slander attributed to the Mighty Book, which has a global fame that cannot be hidden from Easterner or Westerner. Every contemplating researcher observes that the Noble Quran did not flatter a polytheist, a Jew, a Christian, or anyone else in a Meccan or Medinan surah, nor did the tone of its speech differ in pointing out the errors of the Jews or others according to whether the surahs were Meccan or Medinan.

However, since the Quranic verses were revealed gradually (نجوما) according to the occurrence of events related to the religious call, and the trial with the matter of the Jews was after the Migration (الهجرة), the addressing of their affairs and the clarification of the severity regarding them was necessarily in the verses revealed within the Medinan surahs—just like the details of the legislated rulings that were revealed therein according to the pressing need arising from events.

As for what they claimed regarding the exclusivity of the discourse on the connection of Ishmael to Abraham (peace be upon him), the building of the Kaaba, and the establishment of the Hanifi religion to the Medinan surahs, it is refuted by the saying of the Exalted in Surah Ibrahim, which is Meccan, in what He narrated of the prayer of Abraham (peace be upon him): **"When Abraham said, ‘My Lord! Make this city a sanctuary, and save me and my children from worshipping idols... Our Lord! I have settled some of my descendants in a barren valley near Your Sacred House, our Lord, that they may maintain the prayer. So make the hearts of some people fond of them, and provide them with fruits, so that they may give thanks... All praise belongs to Allah, who, despite [my] old age, gave me Ishmael and Isaac. Indeed my Lord hears all supplications.’"** [Qarai: 14:35-39]. Its like has already passed in the verses quoted from Surah al-Saffat just now, informing of the story of the one to be sacrificed.

As for what they mentioned regarding the Judaism of Abraham (peace be upon him), the Quran refutes it by the saying of the Exalted: **"O People of the Book! Why do you argue concerning Abraham, when the Torah and the Evangel were not sent down until after him? Do you not exercise your reason?... Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but he was a Hanif, a Muslim, and he was not one of the polytheists."** [Qarai: 3:65-67].

**The Second Objection:** That the Sabians (الصابئين)—who are the star-worshippers whose gods the Quran mentions Abraham (peace be upon him) addressing in His saying: **"When night darkened over him, he saw a star and said, ‘This is my Lord’"** [Qarai: 6:76] until the end of the verses—were only in the city of Harran, to which Abraham (peace be upon him) migrated from Babylon or from "Ur." This necessitates that his argument with the star-worshippers occurred some time after his argument with the idol-worshippers, his breaking of the idols, and his entering the fire. This does not align with what is apparent from the verses: that the story of the argument with the idol-worshippers and the star-worshippers all occurred within two days at the beginning of his confrontation with his father and his people, as previously explained.

**I say:** This is in reality an objection to the interpretation (التفسير) that was previously presented in the explanation of the verses, not to the Book itself.

Nevertheless, it contains an oversight regarding what history establishes and what sound reasoning provides. As for reasoning: a kingdom that adopts in some of its great cities one of the widespread, known religions—like Sabianism, which was then among the known religions in the world—is not free from its prevalence in the rest of its cities and the presence of a group of its adherents scattered throughout its regions.

As for history: it mentions its prevalence just like the prevalence of idolatry in Babylon, and the existence of many temples therein built in the names of the stars with idols for them erected inside. It is mentioned in the history of the land of Babylon and its surroundings that the building of the temple of the sun-god and the moon-god occurred around the year 3200 BCE. In the stele of the Code of Hammurabi, the sun-god and the moon-god are mentioned, which is close to the time of the Friend Abraham (peace be upon him).

It has already been mentioned in what we quoted from the book *The Remaining Signs* by Abu Rayhan al-Biruni (1): that Budasaf appeared after one year of the reign of Tahmuras in the land of India, brought the Persian script, and called to the

creed of the Sabians, and many people followed him. The Pishdadian kings and some of the Kayanids who inhabited Balkh used to magnify the two luminaries (the sun and moon), the stars, and the universal elements until the time of the appearance of Zoroaster after thirty years of the reign of Vishtaspa.

He continued the discourse until he said: "They attribute governance to the celestial sphere and its bodies, and they speak of their life, speech, hearing, and sight. They magnify the lights. Among their remains is the dome above the mihrab at the enclosure of the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus; it was their place of prayer. The Greeks and Romans were upon their religion. Then it came into the hands of the Jews, who made it their synagogue. Then the Christians overcame them and turned it into a church until Islam and its people came and took it as a mosque.

They had temples and idols in the names of the sun with known shapes, as mentioned by Abu Ma'shar al-Balkhi in his book on houses of worship, such as the temple of Baalbek which belonged to the idol of the sun, and Harran which is attributed to the moon, its construction being in its image like a mantle (طيلسان). Near it is a village called Salamsin, its ancient name being 'Idol of Sin,' meaning the idol of the moon; and another village called Tar' 'Uz, meaning the Gate of Venus.

They mention that the Kaaba and its idols belonged to them, and its worshippers were among them; that al-Lat was in the name of Saturn, and al-Uzza in the name of Venus." End of his words.

Al-Mas'udi mentioned that the doctrine of the Sabians was a type of transformation and evolution within the religion of idolatry, and that Sabianism might transform into idolatry due to the proximity of their origins. Idolatry might involve worshipping the idols of the sun, moon, Venus, and other stars to seek proximity through them to their gods, and then to the God of gods.

He said in *The Meadows of Gold*: "Many of the people of India, China, and other groups believed that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is a body, and that the angels are bodies with specific measures, and that Allah, the Exalted, and His angels are veiled by the heaven. This prompted them to take statues and idols in the image of the Creator, the Mighty and Majestic, and some in the image of the angels with different statures and shapes. Some were in the image of humans and others in different forms, which they worshipped, offered sacrifices to, and made vows for, because of their resemblance—in their view—to the Creator, the Exalted, and their proximity to Him.

They remained upon this for a period of time and a number of ages until some of their sages alerted them that the celestial spheres and stars are the closest visible bodies to Allah, the Exalted, and that they are living and speaking, and that the angels move between them and Allah, and that everything that happens in this world is only according to what the stars bring about by the command of Allah. So they magnified them and offered sacrifices to them so they might benefit them, and they remained upon that for an age."

When they saw that the stars were hidden during the day, and at certain times of the night due to atmospheric veils, some of the sages among them commanded that idols and statues be fashioned according to their forms and shapes. Thus, they made idols and statues corresponding to the number of the great and famous stars. Each group among them would venerate a specific star, offering it a type of sacrifice (القربان) different from the others, believing that if they venerated the fashioned idols, the celestial bodies of the seven planets would move in their favor according to their desires. They built a separate house and temple (هيكل) for each idol and named those temples after those stars.

Some people have even suggested that the Sacred House (البيت الحرام) is the House of Saturn (زحل). According to them, the reason this House has endured through the passage of ages, remaining venerated across all eras, is that it is the House of Saturn, and that Saturn took charge of it. This is because Saturn’s nature is endurance and stability; thus, whatever belongs to it neither vanishes nor perishes, nor does its veneration cease. They mentioned other matters which we have refrained from citing due to the repulsiveness of their description.

As time passed, they worshipped the idols on the basis that they would bring them closer to Allah, and they abandoned the direct worship of the stars. They remained in this state until Budasaf (يوذاسف) appeared in the land of India; he was an Indian. Budasaf traveled from India to Sindh, then to the lands of Sijistan and Zabulistan—which were the lands of Firuz ibn Kabk—then entered Sindh again, and finally reached Kerman.

He claimed prophethood and asserted that he was a messenger of Allah and a medium (واسطة) between Allah and His creation. He arrived in the land of Persia during the early reign of Tahmuras, the King of Persia; it is also said this occurred during the reign of Jam. He was the first to manifest the doctrines of the Sabians (الصابئة) according to what we have previously presented in this book.

Budasaf had commanded the people toward asceticism (الزهد) in this world and to occupy themselves with the higher realms, since the origin of souls (النفوس) is from there and to those realms the return (الصدر) from this world occurs. Budasaf renewed the worship of idols and prostration to them among the people to serve as reminders of their likenesses, and he made their worship acceptable to their intellects through various types of ruses and deceptions.

Those expert in the affairs of this world and the history of its kings mentioned that King Jam was the first to venerate fire and call people to its veneration. He said that it resembles the light of the sun and the stars, for in his view, light (النور) was superior to darkness (الظلمة), and he established ranks for light. Subsequently, people disputed after him, and each group venerated what they deemed worthy of veneration among things, seeking proximity to Allah thereby.

Al-Mas'udi then mentioned the houses they held in veneration, which were seven: the Kaaba, the Sacred House (البيت الحرام), in the name of Saturn; a house on Mount Mars in Isfahan; the House of Mandusan in the land of India; the House of Nawbahar in the city of Balkh in the name of the Moon; the House of Ghamdan in the city of Sana'a in Yemen in the name of Venus; the House of Kawusan in the city of Fergana in the name of the Sun; and a house in the upper regions of China in the name of the First Cause (العلة الأولى).

The Greeks, the ancient Romans, and the Slavs also had venerated houses, some of which were built in the name of the stars, such as the house in Tunis belonging to the Romans, which was in the name of Venus.

Al-Mas'udi further mentioned that the Sabians of Harran (١) had temples (هياكل) named after the intellectual substances (الجواهر العقلية) and the stars. Among these were the Temple of the First Cause (العلة الأولى) and the Temple of the Intellect (العقل). He said: "Among the temples of the Sabians are the Temple of the Chain, the Temple of the Form, and the Temple of the Soul; these are circular in shape. The Temple of Saturn is hexagonal, the Temple of Jupiter is triangular, the Temple of Mars is an oblong rectangle, the Temple of the Sun is square, the Temple of Mercury is triangular, the Temple of Venus is a triangle inside an oblong rectangle, and the Temple of the Moon is octagonal. The Sabians have symbols and secrets regarding what we have mentioned which they keep hidden." End quote. A similar account is found in *al-Milal wa al-Nihal* by al-Shahrastani.

It has become clear from what we have quoted: First, that just as paganism (الوثنية) involved worshipping idols of the deities and the lords of the species (أرباب الأنواع), it also involved worshipping the idols of the stars, the sun, and the moon, and they possessed temples in their names. It is possible that the disputation (حجاج) of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) regarding the star, the moon, and the sun was with the pagans who worshipped them and sought proximity through them, rather than the Sabians. It is also possible that it was with some of the Sabians in the city of Babylon, the town of Ur, or Kutharya, according to some of the narrations cited previously.

Furthermore, the apparent meaning of what the Noble Quran relates is that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) argued with his father and his people and endured their harm for the sake of Allah until he withdrew from them and left them, migrating from their land to the Holy Land, without first traveling from their land to Harran and then from Harran to the Holy Land. That which history books have recorded regarding his migration to Harran first and then to the Holy Land has no source other than the Torah or unreliable reports influenced by Israelite narrations (نفثة إسرائيلية), as is evident to anyone who contemplates the history of al-Tabari and others.

Moreover, some have mentioned that the Harran mentioned in the Torah was a town near Babylon between the Euphrates and Khabur, and it is not the Harran located near Damascus that exists today. (١) Yes, al-Mas'udi mentioned that what remained of their—the Sabians'—venerated temples at that time—which was the year 332 AH—was a house of theirs in the city of Harran at the Gate of Raqqa known as "Maghlitiya," which they consider to be the temple of Azar, the father of Ibrahim the Friend (peace be upon him). These people have much to say regarding Azar and his son Ibrahim. End quote. However, there is no proof in their claims for anything.

Secondly: Just as paganism (الوثنية) sometimes worshipped the sun, the moon, and the stars, the Sabians likewise built houses and temples for the worship of things other than the stars, the moon, and the sun—such as the First Cause (العلة الأولى), the Intellect (العقل), and the Soul (النفس)—and they sought proximity to them just as the pagans did. Herodotus mentioned in his history, while describing the Temple of Babylon, that it consisted of eight towers built one upon another, and that the last and highest tower contained a spacious dome in which there was nothing but a great throne, in front of which was a golden table. There were no statues or idols in the dome, and no one spent the night there except a woman whom the people claimed Allah had chosen for service and appointed for attendance. End quote. (٢) Perhaps this was for the First Cause (العلة الأولى), which is transcendent (المنزهة) above forms and shapes, even if they sometimes depicted it with images they imagined, as mentioned by al-Mas'udi. It has been established that their philosophers used to transcend Allah the Exalted above corporeal forms (الهيئات الجسمانية), material shapes, and positions, describing Him with attributes befitting Him. However, they used to fear the common folk and would not manifest what they believed regarding Him, the Glorified—either because the commoners' understanding was not prepared to receive that, or for political purposes and objectives that necessitated concealing the truth.
